html:
[1]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 古今小說家言 - Refers to the general body of classical Chinese novels, emphasizing their historical span.
  • 指不胜偻 - Hyperbolic expression meaning 'too many to count,' used to indicate the vast number of novels.
  • 饾饤人物 - Literally 'arranging figures like a fruit platter,' metaphorically meaning to piece together characters in a disjointed or superficial manner.
  • 補缀欣戚 - Means 'to patch together joys and sorrows,' referring to the piecing together of emotional elements in a story.
  • 累牍连篇 - Means 'many pages long,' emphasizing the extensive length of the novels.
  • 徒得其似而未传其真 - Means 'only captures their semblance but not their true essence,' criticizing superficial representation.
  • 强笑不欢，强哭不戚 - Means 'forced laughter is not joyful, forced tears are not sorrowful,' criticizing the lack of genuine emotion in the stories.
  • 烏足令耽奇揽异之士心开神释、色飞眉舞哉 - Means 'how could it satisfy the curiosity and delight of those who seek the strange and extraordinary,' expressing disappointment in the lack of engaging content.
  • 天壤间灏气卷舒 - Refers to the vast universe and its dynamic forces, emphasizing the complexity and variety of the world.
  • 鼓荡激薄 - Means 'agitated and stirred,' describing the dynamic and turbulent nature of the world.
  • 变态万状 - Means 'a myriad of transformations,' emphasizing the diversity and complexity of events.
  • 荒诞奇僻 - Means 'absurd and peculiar,' describing unusual and extraordinary events.
  • 可喜可愕可歌可泣 - Means 'joyful, startling, worthy of song, and worthy of tears,' describing a range of emotional responses.
  • 古之所有不必今之所无、古之所无忽为今之所有 - Means 'what was possible in the past is not necessarily impossible today, and what was impossible in the past may be possible today,' emphasizing the potential for new and unexpected events.
  • 飞仙盗侠、牛鬼蛇神 - Refers to mythological and fantastical beings, such as flying immortals, thieves, demons, and ghosts.
  • 虞初一书 - Refers to the *Yu Chou's New Collection*, a collection of strange stories.
  • 汤临川称为小说家之珍珠船 - Means 'Tang Lin-chuan called it the *Pearl Ship* of the novelists,' praising the book's value.
  • 点校之以传世 - Means 'editing and annotating it for posterity,' emphasizing the book's enduring significance.
  • 洵有取尔也 - Means 'indeed, it has its merits,' expressing approval.
  • 原本所撰述，尽摭唐人轶事 - Means 'the original work compiled mostly anecdotes from the Tang Dynasty,' indicating the content of the book.
  • 唐以后无闻焉 - Means 'little is known about it after the Tang Dynasty,' indicating a gap in the book's coverage.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 虞初 - Refers to Yu Chou, a legendary figure known for collecting strange stories.
  • 汤临川 - Refers to Tang Lin-chuan, a scholar who praised the *Yu Chou's New Collection*.
  • 珍珠船 - A metaphorical title for a collection of valuable stories, comparing it to a ship carrying precious pearls.
  • 夷坚 - Refers to the *Yi Jian Zhi*, a collection of strange stories compiled by Zhang Yingyu during the Song Dynasty.
  • 艳异 - Refers to the *Yan Yi Zhi*, another collection of strange stories.

Proper Nouns

  • 虞初 - Yu Chou, a legendary figure known for collecting strange stories.
  • 汤临川 - Tang Lin-chuan, a scholar who praised the *Yu Chou's New Collection*.
  • 珍珠船 - A metaphorical title for a collection of valuable stories.
  • 夷坚 - The *Yi Jian Zhi*, a collection of strange stories compiled by Zhang Yingyu during the Song Dynasty.
  • 艳异 - The *Yan Yi Zhi*, another collection of strange stories.

[2]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 临川续之 - Refers to the continuation of a work by someone from the Linchuan region, likely a specific author or compiler.
  • 调笑滑稽、离奇诡异 - Describes the content as humorous, witty, strange, and bizarre.
  • 引人着胜 - Means 'attracting people to enjoy' or 'drawing people to delight.'
  • 简帙无多，搜采未广 - Indicates that the work is not extensive and the collection is not broad.
  • 慨然有虞初后志之辑 - Expresses a sense of determination to compile a work similar to the *Yu Chou Hou Zhi* (A Collection of Strange Tales from the Later Yu Chou).
  • 授梓问世 - Means 'to publish and make known to the world.'
  • 事多近代也，其文多时贤也 - Indicates that the stories are mostly modern and the authors are contemporary scholars.
  • 事奇而核，文隽而工 - Describes the stories as strange yet factual and the writing as elegant and skillful.
  • 写照传神，彷摹毕肖 - Means 'capturing the essence and spirit, imitating perfectly.'
  • 古有而今不必无、古无而今不必不有 - Suggests that what was possible in ancient times is still possible today, and what was impossible then might be possible now.
  • 且为理之所无，竟为事之所有者 - Indicates that the stories are beyond reason but are real.
  • 读之令人无端而喜、无端而愕、无端而欲歌欲泣 - Describes the emotional impact of reading the stories.
  • 诚得其真，而非仅得其似也夫 - Means 'truly capturing the essence, not just the appearance.'
  • 岂强笑不欢强哭不戚、饾饤补缀之稗官小说可同日语哉 - Questions whether the stories can be compared to forced laughter and crying, or patchwork novels.
  • 学士大夫酬应之余、伊吾之暇 - Refers to scholars and officials during their leisure time.
  • 取是篇而浏览之 - Means 'to read this piece.'
  • 匪惟涤烦祛倦，抑且纵横俯仰，开拓心胸，具达观而发旷怀也已 - Indicates that reading the piece not only relieves boredom and fatigue but also broadens the mind and fosters a broad perspective.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 临川 - Refers to the Linchuan region, known for its literary tradition.
  • 虞初后志之辑 - Refers to the *Yu Chou Hou Zhi* (A Collection of Strange Tales from the Later Yu Chou), a famous collection of strange tales.
  • 康熙癸亥 - Refers to the year 1693 in the reign of Emperor Kangxi.
  • 心斋张潮 - The author, Zhang Chao, who used the pseudonym Xinzhai.

Proper Nouns

  • 临川 - The Linchuan region.
  • 虞初后志之辑 - The *Yu Chou Hou Zhi* (A Collection of Strange Tales from the Later Yu Chou).
  • 康熙癸亥 - The year 1693.
  • 心斋张潮 - The author, Zhang Chao, under the pseudonym Xinzhai.

[3]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 凡例 - A preface or introduction to a collection of writings, often outlining the scope and criteria for inclusion.
  • 文人锐志钻研 - Scholars with sharp ambitions to study deeply.
  • 经传子史 - The classics, commentaries, works of the Hundred Schools of Thought, and historical records.
  • 学士驰情渔猎 - Scholars who pursue knowledge widely and extensively.
  • 世说稗官 - Stories and anecdotes, often from informal sources.
  • 短咏长歌允称游戏 - Short poems and long songs that can be considered playful or recreational.
  • 填词杂剧备极滑稽 - Dramatic works and plays that are highly comedic.
  • 数见而不鲜 - Something that has been seen many times and is no longer novel.
  • 常谈而多复 - Common sayings that are repeated often.
  • 兹集仿虞初之选辑 - This collection imitates the selection and compilation of *Suyu Zhi*.
  • 任诞矜奇 - Being unrestrained and proud of being unique.
  • 搜神拈异 - Searching for and highlighting the supernatural and unusual.
  • 绝不雷同 - Not at all similar or repetitive.
  • 旧调翻新 - Revitalizing old themes with new interpretations.
  • 后来居上 - Those who come later surpass those who came before.
  • 虞初志原本不载选者姓名 - The original *Suyu Zhi* does not record the names of the selectors.
  • 汤临川续编未传作者氏号 - *Tang Linchuan*’s continuation did not pass down the names of the authors.
  • 委宛余编 - Supplementary materials or additional records.
  • 衣黄承辎 - Wearing yellow robes and riding in a carriage.
  • 采访天下异闻 - Collecting unusual news from around the world.
  • 齐谐以为名 - Named after *Qi Hé*, a book of strange tales.
  • 集异之书 - A book that collects unusual stories.
  • 夷坚而著号 - Named after *Yijian*, a book of strange stories.
  • 一切选家 - All selectors or editors.
  • 作者年代为准 - Based on the author's era.
  • 百凡评次 - All evaluations and rankings.
  • 事时世为衡 - Based on the context and time period of the events.
  • 追溯三代以前 - Tracing back to before the Three Dynasties.
  • 一字曰汉 - One character means 'Han.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 虞初 - A figure from the Han Dynasty known for collecting unusual stories.
  • 汤临川 - A reference to Tang Xianzu, a famous playwright and scholar of the Ming Dynasty.
  • 虞初志 - A collection of strange tales compiled during the Han Dynasty.
  • 世说稗官 - Stories and anecdotes, often from informal sources, which were popular in Chinese literature.
  • 齐谐 - A book of strange tales, similar to *Suyu Zhi*.
  • 夷坚 - Another book of strange tales, also known as *Yijian*.
  • 经传子史 - The Four Books and Five Classics, along with works of the Hundred Schools of Thought and historical records, forming the core of classical Chinese literature.
  • 汤临川 - Tang Xianzu, a prominent playwright and scholar from the Ming Dynasty, known for his contributions to Chinese drama and literature.

Proper Nouns

  • 虞初 - A figure from the Han Dynasty known for collecting unusual stories.
  • 汤临川 - A reference to Tang Xianzu, a famous playwright and scholar of the Ming Dynasty.
  • 虞初志 - A collection of strange tales compiled during the Han Dynasty.
  • 齐谐 - A book of strange tales, similar to *Suyu Zhi*.
  • 夷坚 - Another book of strange tales, also known as *Yijian*.
  • 经传子史 - The Four Books and Five Classics, along with works of the Hundred Schools of Thought and historical records, forming the core of classical Chinese literature.
  • 汤临川 - Tang Xianzu, a prominent playwright and scholar from the Ming Dynasty, known for his contributions to Chinese drama and literature.

[4]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 一事而两见者 - Refers to a situation where the same event is described in two different texts.
  • 叙事固无异同 - Indicates that the core content is consistent.
  • 行文必有详略 - Suggests that the style of presentation can vary.
  • 真如赵璧隋珠，不相上下 - Metaphors for valuable items, comparing the quality of the two texts.
  • 顾魏详而王略 - Contrasts the detailed style of Wei with the brief style of Wang.
  • 只期便于览观，非敢意为轩轾 - Explains the purpose of the selection and the neutral stance.
  • 赖古堂藏弆结邻诸选，汇其人之文，专系于姓名之下 - Describes the organization of the Lai Gu Tang collection.
  • 蜩寄斋尺牍新语三编，别其文之类，分叙于卷页之中 - Describes the organization of the Tiao Ji Zhai collection.
  • 今兹选错综无次，庶不涉于拘牵 - Describes the current selection and its flexibility.
  • 且其事荒诞不经，无庸分夫门类 - Describes the nature of the content and the lack of need for classification.
  • 读书之暇，展卷尽可怡神 - Describes the benefit of reading.
  • 倦息之余，披翻自能豁目 - Further describes the benefit of reading.
  • 序爵序齿，从来选政所无 - Describes the absence of a traditional method of organization.
  • 或后或先，总以邮简为次 - Describes the variability in order and the method of organization.
  • 不能虚简以待，亦难缩地以求 - Describes the challenges of the process.
  • 随到随评，即付剞劂之手 - Describes the immediate review and production process.
  • 投函投刺，勿烦酬酢之劳 - Describes the method of communication and the simplicity of the process.
  • 次第未可拘拘，知交定称尔尔 - Describes the flexibility and acceptance of the process.
  • 文自昭明而后始有选名，书从匡郑以来渐多笺释 - Describes the historical development of literature and annotations.
  • 盖由流连欣赏，随手腕以加评 - Describes the reason for annotations and the method of annotation.
  • 抑且阐发揄扬，并胸怀而迸露 - Describes the purpose and depth of the annotations.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 魏叔子 - Wei Shuzi, a historical figure known for his writings.
  • 王不庵 - Wang Bu'an, another historical figure known for his writings.
  • 赵璧隋珠 - Metaphors for valuable items, referring to the quality of the texts.
  • 赖古堂 - A specific collection or anthology.
  • 蜩寄斋 - Another specific collection or anthology.
  • 昭明 - Refers to the period of Zhaoming, a historical point in literature.
  • 匡郑 - Refers to the period of Kuang Zheng, a historical point in literature.

Proper Nouns

  • 魏叔子 - Wei Shuzi
  • 王不庵 - Wang Bu'an
  • 赵璧隋珠 - Zhao Bi and Sui Zhu
  • 赖古堂 - Lai Gu Tang
  • 蜩寄斋 - Tiao Ji Zhai
  • 昭明 - Zhaoming
  • 匡郑 - Kuang Zheng

[5]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 兹集触目赏心 - Pleasing to the eye and heart; visually and emotionally pleasing.
  • 漫附数言 - Casually add a few words; hastily append a few remarks.
  • 挥毫拍案 - Writing or composing with a brush; metaphor for expressing strong emotion or admiration.
  • 忽加赘语 - Suddenly added superfluous remarks; unexpectedly appended extraneous words.
  • 忼慨激昂 - Passionate and stirring; emotionally charged.
  • 咨嗟唱叹 - To sigh and lament; to express admiration through exclamations.
  • 敢谓发明 - Dare to say it's an elucidation; dare to claim it's an insight.
  • 聊抒兴趣 - Merely to express one’s interest.
  • 既自怡悦 - Having brought oneself pleasure.
  • 愿共讨论 - I wish to share this with you for discussion.
  • 幽奇 - Mysterious and strange; recondite and unusual.
  • 衷多感愤 - In my heart, I have much sorrow and indignation.
  • 四怀 - Four themes or categories of emotional or literary expression.
  • 写心一启 - To express the heart and open the mind.
  • 秘本 - Rare or secret editions.
  • 不惮假抄 - Not afraid to borrow and copy.
  • 异书 - Unusual or rare books.
  • 辄为求购 - Immediately seek to purchase.
  • 第愧搜罗未广 - However, I feel ashamed; my collection is not yet extensive.
  • 尤惭辨辑无多 - Especially ashamed that my critical editing is limited.
  • 速祈惠教 - Please quickly offer your guidance.
  • 乞邻而与 - To ask neighbors for help; to seek assistance from those nearby.
  • 无妨举尔所知 - Do not hesitate to share what you know.
  • 表彰轶事 - To highlight unusual or rare stories.
  • 传布奇文 - To disseminate extraordinary writings.
  • 借迳沽名 - Using a path to gain fame; using a method to seek reputation.
  • 居奇射利 - To hoard rare items and seek profit.
  • 素不相知 - Have never known each other before.
  • 授梓 - The act of printing or publishing a book.
  • 旧识 - Old acquaintances.
  • 剞劂 - The carving of woodblocks for printing.
  • 梨枣 - A metaphor for printing materials (woodblocks for printing).
  • 浮沉 - To be delayed or held back.
  • 珠玉 - Precious writings; fine works.
  • 海内名家 - Renowned scholars or writers within the country.
  • 未传之作 - Works not yet published or disseminated.
  • 坊间 - Bookshops; commercial publishers.
  • 定本 - Definitive editions; standard editions.
  • 数见之书 - Books rarely seen; rare books.
  • 幽人 - Reclusive scholars; those who seek the esoteric.
  • 素嗜探奇 - Has always loved exploring the strange.
  • 尤耽考异 - Especially enjoys examining differences; especially enjoys textual criticism.
  • 嗣选 - Subsequent selections; future selections.
  • 古世说 - A type of classical text containing anecdotes or sayings.
  • 古文尤雅 - Especially elegant ancient prose.
  • 古文辞法传集 - A collection of ancient prose styles or methods.
  • 布粟集 - A title of a book; likely a collection of essays or anecdotes.
  • 壮游便览 - A guide to grand travels; a collection of travel notes.
  • 次第告竣 - Will be completed in order.
  • 就正有道 - To seek correction from the wise.
  • 缪盭 - Errors or mistakes.
  • 幸赐教言 - Kindly offer your guidance.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 四怀诗 - A set of poems based on four themes or emotional categories, likely related to the author’s personal interests (e.g., heroes, beauties, immortals, etc.).
  • 征选外史启 - A call for submissions or a notice inviting contributions to a compilation of 'outer histories' or 'unofficial histories.'
  • 幽人 - Reclusive scholars or those who pursue esoteric knowledge, often associated with Confucian or Daoist traditions.
  • 考异 - Textual criticism or the examination of variant readings in classical texts.
  • 古世说 - A classical text containing anecdotes or sayings from ancient times.
  • 古文辞法传集 - A collection of prose styles or methods from ancient times, possibly a guide to classical writing.
  • 布粟集 - A title of a book; possibly a collection of essays or anecdotes.
  • 壮游便览 - A guide or collection of travel notes; likely inspired by the tradition of literary travel writing.

[6]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 心斋主人 - Title suggesting a person of deep introspection or spiritual cultivation.
  • 广陵之诒清堂 - The Tranquil Hall of Guangling, a specific location in Guangling (modern-day Yangzhou, Jiangsu).
  • 非穷愁不能著书 - Idiom meaning 'without hardship and sorrow, one cannot write books,' emphasizing the idea that adversity inspires literary creation.
  • 如怨如慕、如泣如诉 - Poetic expression meaning 'like lamentation and longing, like weeping and pleading,' describing the emotional tone of the writings.
  • 误堕坑阱中 - Metaphor for falling into a difficult situation or misfortune.
  • 肺附中山 - Metaphorical expression possibly referring to a severe illness or affliction, though the exact meaning is unclear and may be idiomatic.
  • 藉书而释 - To find relief through books, suggesting that reading serves as a coping mechanism for hardship.
  • 居安处顺 - Living in peace and comfort, contrasting with the state of 'poverty and sorrow.'
  • 焚香静读 - To burn incense and read quietly, a traditional practice of reading in a serene and focused manner.
  • 俾世之读我书者 - To enable those who read my books, indicating the author's intention for his readers to understand his circumstances.
  • 有心人 - A person with compassion, referring to someone who would sympathize with the author's plight.
  • 康熙庚辰初夏三在道人张潮识 - The author's signature, indicating that Zhang Chao, a Daoist, wrote this in the early summer of the year Gengchen during the Kangxi era (1660).

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 广陵 - The ancient name for Yangzhou, a city in Jiangsu Province, known for its cultural and literary heritage.
  • 诒清堂 - A specific location, likely a study or retreat, associated with Zhang Chao.
  • 穷愁 - A term often used in Chinese literature to describe a state of poverty and sorrow, which is believed to inspire creative works.
  • 聂隐娘 - A legendary figure from Chinese folklore, often associated with supernatural abilities and martial arts.
  • 羼提波罗密 - A Buddhist concept emphasizing patience and forbearance, suggesting that the author is practicing this virtue in the face of his difficulties.
  • 康熙庚辰 - The year 1660 in the reign of the Kangxi Emperor, a significant period in Chinese history.
  • 张潮 - The author, a Daoist and scholar known for his literary works.

Proper Nouns

  • 广陵 - The ancient name for Yangzhou.
  • 诒清堂 - The specific location where Zhang Chao wrote this text.
  • 聂隐娘 - A legendary figure from Chinese folklore.
  • 羼提波罗密 - A Buddhist concept.
  • 康熙庚辰 - The year 1660 in the Kangxi era.
  • 张潮 - The author of the text.

[7]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 卷 - A traditional unit of organization for Chinese literature, often used to denote a collection or anthology.
  • 传 - A biographical or narrative genre, often used to record the lives of notable individuals or recount significant events.
  • 自序 - An introductory section written by the author, typically providing context or background for the work.
  • 记 - A genre that records events, people, or places, often with a focus on historical or anecdotal details.
  • 外传 - A supplementary or additional narrative, often providing more detailed or alternative accounts.
  • 小传 - A brief biography or short narrative, often focusing on a single individual or event.

[8]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 卷 - Refers to a volume or chapter of a book, often used in the context of classical Chinese literature.
  • 传 - Means 'transmission' or 'biography,' used here to denote a collection of biographical or narrative texts.
  • 逸事 - Means 'anecdotes' or 'stories of extraordinary events,' often used to describe the lives of notable individuals.
  • 墓志铭 - Refers to a 'tombstone inscription,' a type of memorial text written in honor of the deceased.
  • 五人传 - Means 'Biographies of Five People,' likely a collection of short biographies or narratives.
  • 小传 - Refers to a 'short biography,' a brief narrative about a person's life.
  • 鬼孝子传 - Means 'Biography of a Filial Ghost,' a narrative about a ghost who demonstrates filial piety.
  • 奇女子传 - Refers to 'Biography of a Remarkable Woman,' a narrative about a woman with extraordinary qualities.
  • 曲全节义疏 - Means 'Explanation of the Complete Loyalty and Righteousness in a Play,' likely a commentary on a theatrical work.
  • 再来诗谶记 - Means 'Record of the Poetic Prophecy of Reincarnation,' a narrative about a poetic prophecy related to reincarnation.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 吴伟业 - A renowned poet and writer of the Ming and Qing dynasties, known for his contributions to Chinese literature.
  • 孙文正、黄石斋 - Historical figures celebrated for their integrity and virtue, often featured in classical Chinese literature.
  • 箫洞虚 - A character or historical figure whose life or deeds are recounted in the text.
  • 鬼孝子 - A supernatural figure who demonstrates filial piety, a common theme in Chinese folklore.
  • 黄履庄 - A character or historical figure whose life or deeds are recounted in the text.
  • 戚三郎 - A historical figure whose story is recounted in the text, likely a notable individual from the past.
  • 象记 - A narrative or description related to elephants, possibly a historical or mythological account.
  • 纪周侍御事 - A narrative about Zhou Shi, possibly a government official, whose affairs are recorded.
  • 姚江神灯记 - A narrative about supernatural lights in Yaojiang, possibly a local legend or myth.
  • 记盗 - A narrative about bandits or criminal activities, often found in historical or literary texts.
  • 化虎记 - A narrative about someone transforming into a tiger, a common theme in Chinese folklore.
  • 奇女子 - A character or historical figure, possibly a woman with extraordinary qualities, whose life or deeds are recounted in the text.
  • 江石芸 - A character or historical figure whose life or deeds are recounted in the text.
  • 耕云子 - A character or figure associated with cloud plowing, possibly a metaphor for a certain lifestyle or profession.
  • 孝贼传 - A narrative about a thief who demonstrates filial piety, a paradoxical but common theme in Chinese literature.
  • 王翠翘 - A character or historical figure whose life or deeds are recounted in the text.
  • 戴文进 - A character or historical figure whose life or deeds are recounted in the text.
  • 髯樵 - A character or historical figure, likely a woodcutter with a beard, whose life or deeds are recounted in the text.
  • 赵希乾 - A character or historical figure whose life or deeds are recounted in the text.
  • 万夫雄打虎传 - A narrative about a hero who slays a tiger, a common theme in Chinese folklore.
  • 剑侠传 - A narrative about a hero skilled in swordsmanship, a common theme in Chinese literature.
  • 皇华纪闻 - A narrative about a royal envoy's experiences, often found in historical or literary texts.
  • 毛女 - A character or historical figure, possibly a woman with unusual features, whose life or deeds are recounted in the text.
  • 宝婺生 - A character or historical figure, possibly a woman of high status or virtue, whose life or deeds are recounted in the text.
  • 王义士 - A character or historical figure who demonstrates righteousness, often celebrated in classical Chinese literature.
  • 纪陆子容事 - A narrative about Lu Zirong, possibly a notable individual from the past.
  • 雌雌儿 - A character or historical figure, likely a girl, whose life or deeds are recounted in the text.
  • 沙张白 - A proper noun referring to Sha Zhangbai, a historical figure or author.

[9]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 卷 - Refers to a section or chapter in a book.
  • 偶筆 - Literally 'casual notes' or 'random writings,' suggesting a collection of informal or personal essays.
  • 傳 - A narrative text, often used for biographies or stories.
  • 記 - A narrative or descriptive text, often recounting events or describing objects.
  • 志 - A record or inscription, often used for memorials or epitaphs.

[10]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 記同夢 - A phrase indicating a record or account of shared dreams.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 為政廉仁 - Means 'to govern with integrity and benevolence.'
  • 十年无所取于民 - Means 'for ten years, he did not take anything from the people.'
  • 不受竿牍 - Literally means 'did not accept bamboo slips,' which refers to not accepting written petitions or requests.
  • 爱民如子，嫉客若仇 - Means 'loving the people like one's own children, hating guests like enemies.'
  • 捐俸请托 - Means 'to donate his salary to make requests.'
  • 免泗州修河夫五百名 - Means 'to exempt 500 workers from the repair work on the river in Sizhou.'
  • 请革过闸粮船纤夫 - Means 'to request the abolition of the practice of using coolies for grain ships passing through the lock.'
  • 著为令 - Means 'to establish it as a regulation.'
  • 旧例 - Means 'the old custom or precedent.'
  • 掣盐封引 - Means 'to draw and seal the salt permits.'
  • 仪征令皆有赂 - Means 'all the officials in Yizheng had bribes.'
  • 独绝之 - Means 'to cut off this practice alone.'
  • 下车日 - Means 'on the day he took office.'
  • 廉得大憝董奇、董九功等 - Means 'to uncover the great evildoers Dong Qi and Dong Jiugong, etc.'
  • 置于法 - Means 'to punish them according to the law.'
  • 窝访之，害遂除 - Means 'to root out the evil.'
  • 间行真州 - Means 'to travel secretly to Zhenzhou.'
  • 听断 - Means 'to hear and decide cases.'
  • 心折 - Means 'to be impressed and convinced.'
  • 改礼部仪制司主事 - Means 'to be promoted to the position of Assistant in the Department of Ceremonies.'
  • 巡抚南直隶朱公大典疏表公贤劳 - Means 'Governor of Nanjing, Zhu Gong Dadian, submitted a memorial praising the official's virtue and diligence.'
  • 上谕一体考选 - Means 'the emperor ordered that he be included in the examination for promotion.'
  • 因目阁臣曰 - Means 'and said to the cabinet ministers.'
  • 有臣如此而不用，朕之过也 - Means 'to have a minister like this and not use him is my fault.'
  • 三月，上御宏政门召见 - Means 'in March, the emperor summoned him at the Hall of Great Government.'
  • 应对称旨 - Means 'his responses pleased the emperor.'
  • 擢礼科给事中 - Means 'to be promoted to the position of Censor in the Department of Rites.'
  • 赐糕果汤饼 - Means 'to be bestowed with cakes, fruits, and noodles.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 公为政廉仁 - Refers to the official's governance characterized by integrity and benevolence.
  • 十年无所取于民 - Indicates a long period of self-restraint and dedication to the welfare of the people.
  • 不受竿牍 - Highlights the official's refusal to accept written petitions, suggesting a strict adherence to ethical standards.
  • 爱民如子，嫉客若仇 - Emphasizes the official's deep care for the people and disdain for those who might exploit them.
  • 捐俸请托 - Shows the official's willingness to sacrifice personal resources for the benefit of the people.
  • 免泗州修河夫五百名 - Demonstrates the official's efforts to alleviate the burden on the people.
  • 请革过闸粮船纤夫 - Indicates the official's initiative to improve the efficiency and fairness of the grain transport system.
  • 著为令 - Suggests the official's ability to implement lasting reforms.
  • 旧例 - Refers to traditional practices that the official sought to change.
  • 掣盐封引 - Points to the official's role in overseeing the salt trade, which was a significant economic activity.
  • 仪征令皆有赂 - Highlights the corruption prevalent among local officials.
  • 独绝之 - Emphasizes the official's unique stance against corruption.
  • 下车日 - Marks the beginning of the official's term in office.
  • 廉得大憝董奇、董九功等 - Indicates the official's effectiveness in uncovering and punishing corrupt officials.
  • 置于法 - Suggests the official's commitment to justice.
  • 窝访之，害遂除 - Emphasizes the official's success in eliminating evil.
  • 间行真州 - Suggests the official's methodical and thorough approach to governance.
  • 听断 - Highlights the official's fairness and competence in judicial matters.
  • 心折 - Indicates the high regard in which the official was held by others.
  • 改礼部仪制司主事 - Marks a significant promotion in the official's career.
  • 巡抚南直隶朱公大典疏表公贤劳 - Shows the official's recognition and praise by a high-ranking governor.
  • 上谕一体考选 - Indicates the emperor's acknowledgment of the official's merits.
  • 因目阁臣曰 - Suggests the official's influence and respect within the imperial court.
  • 有臣如此而不用，朕之过也 - Emphasizes the emperor's regret for not utilizing the official's talents.
  • 三月，上御宏政门召见 - Marks a significant moment in the official's career, where he was summoned by the emperor.
  • 应对称旨 - Indicates the official's ability to meet the emperor's expectations.
  • 擢礼科给事中 - Marks another significant promotion in the official's career.
  • 赐糕果汤饼 - Suggests the emperor's favor and recognition of the official's service.

  • 触首辅怒 - Means 'this angered the chief minister.'
  • 上谕：代人规卸、为人出缺，陛下果何见而云然？ - Means 'The emperor's edict: 'Delegating blame and creating vacancies for others, what do you mean by this, Your Majesty?'
  • 二十四气蜚语，腾闻清禁，此必大奸巨憝恶言官不利于己 - Means 'rumors about the twenty-four solar terms have spread to the inner palace, this must be the work of great traitors who hate the remonstrant officials.'
  • 着革职，锦衣卫拿送北镇抚司打问！ - Means 'Ordering his dismissal, the Imperial Guard to arrest and send him to the Northern Censorate for interrogation!'
  • 行人司熊开元面劾首辅 - Means 'Xiong Kaiyuan, the official of the Imperial Messenger Office, publicly impeached the chief minister.'
  • 既以补牍语不相应，同时下狱，几死，后并得赦 - Means 'After his memorial was found to be inappropriate, he was imprisoned along with [the subject], nearly dying, but was later pardoned.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 初 - Indicates the beginning of an event or situation.
  • 勺水不得入口 - Literally means 'not even a spoonful of water could enter the mouth,' emphasizing severe deprivation.
  • 冰雪交积 - Describes the accumulation of ice and snow, indicating harsh winter conditions.
  • 公僵卧土室 - Means 'the public lay stiff in a dirt room,' describing the harsh conditions of imprisonment.
  • 身婴三木 - Refers to being bound with three wooden restraints, a form of physical punishment.

  • 九卿台省屡疏救，不报 - Indicates that high-ranking officials repeatedly petitioned for the public's release, but their requests were ignored.
  • 此处缺二十二字 - Indicates a missing section of twenty-two characters.
  • 例凡一拶敲五十，一夹敲五十，杖二十 - Describes the standard punishments: one拶 (a type of torture) for fifty blows, one夹 (another type of torture) for fifty blows, and twenty strokes with a stick.

  • 公既备刑 - Indicates that the public had already endured the punishments.

  • 半日稍苏 - Indicates that the public regained consciousness after half a day.
  • 清宏令尉灌酒一杯 - Means 'the clean and generous commandant gave him a cup of wine.'
  • 使毕谳 - Means 'to complete the judgment.'

  • 吾当待尽宣州，以绝吾志 - The subject will wait to die in Xuanchou to end his aspirations, indicating a final act of defiance.
  • 越数日，则曰 - After several days, the subject then said, indicating a change in decision.
  • 吾不能往矣！死必埋我敬亭之麓 - The subject cannot go anymore and will be buried at the foot of Jingting Mountain, indicating a final wish.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 公隱居後，多著述 - Standard classical expression for a scholar's literary output after leaving official life.
  • 绝不示人 - Strong refusal to share his work publicly.
  • 传甲乙以来殉节诸贤曰《正气集》 - Allusion to the Jiayi period (political turmoil during the Ming dynasty), and '殉节' refers to dying for one's moral or political convictions.
  • 自题己亥后诗文曰《餺飥集》 - '餺飥' (xiaohuo) is a type of porridge, used metaphorically to refer to simple or unrefined writings.
  • 皆藏之 - Common classical expression for the act of concealing or preserving texts privately.
  • 公有赠禧序及见怀诸诗，皆未出 - '赠禧序' refers to a preface or dedication written for Hui Xi.
  • 公死，而公二子乃写寄禧山中也 - Typical narrative structure in classical biographies, emphasizing the posthumous transmission of works.
  • 予客吴门，数信宿公 - '信宿' means to stay for two nights, often used to indicate frequent visits.
  • 哀哉！北镇抚司狱廷杖、立枷诸制，此秦法所未有 - Strong critique of Ming-era torture practices.
  • 始作俑者，罪可胜道哉！ - Classical idiom referencing the 'first to use a clay figure' in burial, implying that the originator of an evil practice is most to blame.
  • 谥法：秉德不回曰孝 - Direct quotation from classical *Shi Ji* (Records of the Grand Historian) or *Book of Rites*.
  • 经曰：事君不忠，非孝也 - Reference to Confucian classics, likely the *Analects* or *Book of Rites*.
  • 公死不忘君，全而归之，可以为孝矣，宜谥曰贞孝 - Moral judgment on the subject’s loyalty.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 敬亭集 - A collection of poems and writings by the subject. '敬亭' (Jingting) is likely the name of a mountain or a place associated with the author, possibly the Jingting Mountain in Anhui.
  • 正气集 - A collection of loyal officials who died for their principles. '正气' (zhengqi) refers to righteous or moral energy, often used in Confucian and Neo-Confucian contexts.
  • 餺飥集 - A collection of writings titled after a simple dish (porridge), suggesting the author's modest or unpretentious style.
  • 己亥 - Ji亥 is a sexagenary year (60-year cycle), corresponding to the year 1649 in the Gregorian calendar (during the Ming-Qing transition).
  • 魏禧 - Wei Xi: A scholar and writer of the late Ming and early Qing periods, known as one of the 'Three Huis' (along with Hui Ding and Hui Zhi).
  • 吴门 - Wu Gate (Wuzhen), a name for Suzhou, a cultural and literary center during the Ming and Qing dynasties.
  • 信宿 - To stay for two nights, indicating frequent visits or close relationships.
  • 北镇抚司狱 - Northern Pacification Office prison: A notorious prison in the Ming dynasty known for its harsh treatment of political prisoners.
  • 廷杖 - Tingzhang: A punishment in which officials were beaten with a staff in the imperial court, often used to punish dissent or disloyalty.
  • 立枷 - Standing枷: A torture device where the prisoner is forced to stand for long periods with a wooden frame around the neck and legs, used in Ming prisons.
  • 秦法 - The laws of the Qin dynasty, known for their harshness and centralization.
  • 谥法 - The rules for assigning posthumous titles to officials, based on their moral and political conduct.
  • 贞孝 - Zhenxiao: A posthumous title combining 'zhen' (true, loyal) and 'xiao' (filial), indicating a person who was both loyal to the state and filial to the family.

  • 泯泯然不见功名于世者 - Refers to those who are obscure and fail to achieve fame or success in the world.
  • 岂天之生才，不必为人用与 - Rhetorical question meaning 'Is it that heaven produces talent that is not necessarily meant to be used by others?'

  • 视其貌，当年三十，然则大铁椎今四十耳 - Statement meaning 'Looking at his appearance, he was thirty years old then, so now the Great Iron Mallet is forty years old.'
  • 子灿又尝见其写市物帖子，甚工楷书也 - Statement meaning 'Zi Can also saw him writing a note about market goods, which was very skilled in regular script.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 母遣之出游 - 遣 means 'send,' and 出游 means 'to travel outside,' indicating that the mother sent him out to travel.
  • 每岁三时出游，秋冬觐省，以为常 - 每岁三时 means 'three times a year,' 出游 means 'to travel outside,' 觐省 means 'to visit and pay respects,' and 以为常 means 'to make it a regular practice.'
  • 东南佳山水 - 佳山水 means 'fine landscapes,' referring to beautiful natural scenery.
  • 如东、西洞庭、阳羡、京口、金陵、吴兴、武林、浙西径山、天目、浙东五泄、四明、天台、雁宕、南海、落伽 - These are proper names of famous scenic spots and places in China.
  • 皆几案衣带间物耳 - 几案衣带间物 means 'things within reach of a desk or belt,' suggesting that these places are familiar and easily accessible.
  • 有再三至，有数至，无仅一至者 - 再三至 means 'to visit multiple times,' 数至 means 'to visit several times,' and 无仅一至者 means 'no place was visited only once.'
  • 从一奴，或一僧，一杖，一袱被 - 从 means 'to follow,' 奴 means 'servant,' 僧 means 'monk,' 杖 means 'staff,' and 袱被 means 'bundle of bedding.'
  • 不治装，不裹粮 - 治装 means 'to prepare luggage,' and 裹粮 means 'to pack food.'
  • 能忍饥数日，能遇食即饱 - 忍饥 means 'to endure hunger,' 数日 means 'several days,' 遇食 means 'to find food,' and 即饱 means 'to eat until full.'
  • 能徒步走数百里 - 徒步 means 'to walk on foot,' and 数百里 means 'several hundred miles.'
  • 凌绝壁，冒丛菁，攀援上下，悬度绠汲，捷如青猿，健如黄犊 - 凌绝壁 means 'to climb sheer cliffs,' 冒丛菁 means 'to pass through dense vegetation,' 攀援上下 means 'to climb up and down,' 悬度绠汲 means 'to cross by rope or bamboo pole,' 捷如青猿 means 'as agile as a green monkey,' and 健如黄犊 means 'as strong as a young buffalo.'
  • 以崟岩为床席，以溪涧为饮沐 - 以...为 means 'to use...as,' 崟岩 means 'rocky cliffs,' 溪涧 means 'streams and brooks,' and 饮沐 means 'drinking and washing.'
  • 以山魅木客、王孙玃父为伴侣 - 山魅木客 means 'forest spirits and tree dwellers,' 王孙玃父 means 'princes and ape fathers,' and 伴侣 means 'companions.'
  • 儚儚粥粥，口不能道词 - 儚儚粥粥 describes a state of confusion or dizziness, and 口不能道词 means 'unable to speak coherently.'
  • 与之论山经，辨水脉，拽讨形胜 - 论山经 means 'to discuss the Shan Jing (Classic of Mountains and Seas),' 辨水脉 means 'to analyze water systems,' and 拽讨形胜 means 'to explore geographical features.'
  • 划然心开 - 划然 means 'suddenly,' and 心开 means 'the mind opens.'
  • 居平未尝鞶帨为古文辞 - 居平 means 'in daily life,' 未尝 means 'never,' 鞶帨 means 'to prepare,' and 古文辞 means 'ancient literary works.'
  • 就破壁枯树，燃松拾穗，走笔为记 - 就 means 'to sit by,' 破壁枯树 means 'broken walls and dead trees,' 燃松拾穗 means 'burn pine branches and gather pine cones,' and 走笔为记 means 'to write quickly.'
  • 如甲乙之簿，如丹青之画 - 如甲乙之簿 means 'like a ledger of A and B,' and 如丹青之画 means 'like a painting in red and blue.'
  • 虽才笔之士无以加也 - 虽 means 'even if,' 才笔之士 means 'talented writers,' and 无以加也 means 'there is nothing to add to it.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 游台、宕还 - Elliptical phrase meaning 'after touring the Tai and Dang mountains, he returned.'
  • 质明 - Classical term meaning 'at the break of day' or 'just before dawn.'
  • 唯唯 - Term used to express agreement or assent without elaboration; can be translated as 'nodded in agreement' or 'answered affirmatively.'
  • 取间道 - 'To take a secluded or hidden path,' implying a less-traveled route.
  • 扪萝上龙湫 - 'To climb by grasping vines and climbing up to Longqiu.' '龙湫' refers to a deep pool or waterfall.
  • 有宕焉，雁所家也 - 'There was a cave or ravine there, where geese make their home.' '宕' can mean a cave, ravine, or secluded area.
  • 攀绝磴 - 'To climb steep steps or rocks.' '绝磴' refers to extremely steep or dangerous steps.
  • 白云、云外两僧团瓢 - 'Two monks named Baiyun and Yunwai, who wore round bowls (a term for begging bowls).' '团瓢' is a metaphorical term for a begging bowl.
  • 其颠罡风逼人 - 'At the summit, the fierce wind was so strong it struck one in the face.' '罡风' refers to a strong, piercing wind.
  • 糜鹿数百群 - 'Hundreds of groups of deer and antelope.' '糜' is a type of deer, and '鹿' is deer; the term may refer to a variety of wild animals.
  • 围绕而宿 - 'Surrounded and made their homes.' Implies the animals encircled the traveler and stayed nearby.
  • 三宿而始下 - 'Stayed for three nights and then descended.' '宿' here is used in the sense of spending a night or staying overnight.
  • 争奇逐胜 - 'To compete in seeking out the strange and the beautiful.' A common classical expression referring to the pursuit of scenic and unusual places.
  • 欲赌身命 - 'Willing to wager one's life.' Implies a willingness to take risks for the sake of discovery or adventure.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 丙子九月 - Refers to the month of September in the year of the Fire Rat according to the Chinese sexagenary cycle.
  • 辞家西迈 - Literally 'leave home and move westward,' indicating a journey westward.
  • 僧静闻愿登鸡足礼迦叶 - The monk Jingwen wished to ascend Mount Jizu to pay respects to Kasyapa, a religious pilgrimage.
  • 遇盗于湘江，静闻被创死 - He encountered bandits on the Xiang River, and Jingwen was wounded and died, a narrative of a tragic event.
  • 函其骨，负之以行 - Encased his bones in a box and carried them along, showing a deep sense of respect and loyalty.
  • 泛洞庭，上衡岳，穷七十二峰 - Traversed Dongting Lake, ascended Mount Heng, and explored the seventy-two peaks, describing a series of geographical locations.
  • 再登峨眉，北抵岷山，极于松潘 - Ascended Mount Emei again, traveled north to Mount Min, and reached Songpan, continuing the geographical journey.
  • 又南过大渡河，至黎、雅，登瓦屋、晒经诸山 - Then traveled south over the Dadu River, to Lizi and Yaxi, and climbed Mount Wahu and Mount Shaijing, further detailing the journey.
  • 复寻金沙江，极于犁牛徼外 - Then explored the Jinsha River, reaching beyond the border of Ligniu, indicating a journey to the far reaches.
  • 由金沙南泛澜沧，由澜沧北寻盘江 - From the Jinsha River, he traveled south along the Lancang River, and from the Lancang River, he traveled north along the Pan River, describing a complex river journey.
  • 大约在西南诸夷境，而贵竹、滇南之观，亦几尽矣 - This was approximately in the territory of the southwestern barbarians, and the sights of Gui Zhu and Dian Nan were almost all seen, providing a geographical and cultural context.
  • 过丽江，憩点苍、鸡足，瘗静闻骨于迦叶道场，从宿愿也 - Passed through Lijiang, rested at Diancang and Mount Jizu, and buried Jingwen's bones in Kasyapa's shrine, fulfilling his long-held wish.
  • 由鸡足而西，出玉门关数千里，至昆仑山，穷星宿海，去中夏三万四千三百里 - From Mount Jizu, he traveled west, passing through the Jade Gate Pass for thousands of miles, to Mount Kunlun, and reached the Star Sea, a distance of 34,300 li from Central China.
  • 登半山，风吹衣欲堕，望见外方黄金宝塔 - Climbed halfway up the mountain, where the wind blew his clothes almost off, and he saw a golden stupa in the distance.
  • 又数千里，至西番，参大宝法王 - Traveling another several thousand miles, he reached the Western Regions and met the Great Treasure Dharma King.
  • 鸣沙之外，咸称胡国，如述庐、阿耨诸名，由旬不能悉 - Beyond the singing sands, all lands were called the Huns, such as Shule and Anou, whose names cannot be fully listed.
  • 《西域志》称沙河阻远，望人马积骨为标识，鬼魅热风，无得免者 - According to the *Western Regions Gazetteer*, the sandy river is remote and dangerous, with human and horse bones serving as markers, and no one can escape the ghosts, demons, and hot winds.
  • 玄奘法师受诸魔折，具载本传 - Master Xuanzang suffered from various demons, as detailed in his biography.
  • 霞客信宿往返，如适莽苍 - Xiake stayed for two nights and returned, as if he had been in a vast wilderness.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 還至峨眉山下 - Literal phrase meaning 'returned to the foot of Mount Emei,' indicating a return journey.
  • 托估客附所得奇樹虬根以歸 - Complex phrase meaning 'entrusted a merchant to bring back the strange trees and twisted roots he had obtained,' showing the method of transporting items.
  • 並以《溯江紀源》一編寓予 - Means 'and also gave me a copy of *Sù Jiāng Jì Yuán*,' indicating the gift of a written work.
  • 言《禹貢》岷山導江，乃泛濫中國之始，非發源也 - Statement about the *Yǔ Gòng* (Classic of History) mentioning that the Minshan Mountains are where the Yangtze River begins to flood, not its source.
  • 中國入河之水，為省五；入江之水，為省十一 - Statistical statement about the number of provinces contributing water to the Yellow River and the Yangtze River.
  • 計其吐納，江倍於河 - Means 'calculating the outflow and inflow, the Yangtze is twice as much as the Yellow River.'
  • 按其發源，河自昆侖之北，江亦自昆侖之南，短而河源長也 - Statement about the origins of the rivers, indicating that the Yellow River starts north of Kunlun, and the Yangtze starts south of Kunlun, but the Yellow River's source is longer.
  • 又辨三龍大勢，北龍夾河之北，南龍抱江之南，中龍中界之，特短 - Description of the three dragon-like mountain ranges, with the northern one flanking the Yellow River, the southern one surrounding the Yangtze, and the central one being shorter.
  • 北龍只南向半支入中國，唯南龍磅礴半宇內，其脈亦發於昆侖，與金沙江相並南下，環滇池以達五嶺 - Detailed description of the southern dragon's influence and its connection to the Jinsha River and Dianchi Lake.
  • 龍長則源脈亦長，江之所以大於河也 - Conclusion that the longer the dragon (mountain range), the longer the source and thus the larger the river.
  • 其書數萬言，皆訂補桑經郦注及漢、宋諸儒疏解《禹貢》所未及 - Statement about the book containing tens of thousands of words, correcting and supplementing previous works on the *Yǔ Gòng*.
  • 予撮其大略如此 - Means 'I have summarized the main points here.'
  • 霞客還滇南，足不良行，修《雞足山志》，三月而畢 - Statement about his severe illness and his words to those who visited him.
  • 麗江木太守偫餱糧、具筍舆以歸 - Description of the local governor providing food and bamboo sedan chairs for his return.
  • 病甚，語問疾者曰 - Statement about his severe illness and his words to visitors.
  • 漢張騫鑿空，未睹昆侖 - Reference to Zhang Qian's exploration and his not seeing Kunlun.
  • 吾以老布衣，孤筇雙履，窮河沙，上昆侖，歷西域，題名絕國，與三人而為四，死不恨矣 - Statement about his own journey and the significance of his travels.

  • 于厅事之东北角施八尺屏障 - Means 'in the northeast corner of the hall, a screen eight feet high was placed,' describing the setup for the performance.

  • 若不复与予见者 - 'As if he would never see me again.' '若' means 'as if' or 'like.'
  • 予私以为予当复见之 - 'I privately thought that I would see him again.' '私' means 'privately' or 'in my heart.'
  • 意以其盲而止耳 - 'I thought it was just because he was blind.' '意' means 'thought' or 'intended.'
  • 孰意遂不复见耶 - 'Who would have thought that I would never see him again?' '孰意' means 'who would have thought.'
  • 此公归 - 'This gentleman returned home.'
  • 吾师静原相公方督学江以北 - 'My teacher, Jingyuan Xianggong, was supervising education in the northern part of Jiang.' '督学' means 'supervising education.'
  • 耳其名 - 'He heard of his name.' '耳' means 'to hear of' or 'to know of.'
  • 询之郡大夫 - 'He asked the prefect.' '询' means 'to ask' or 'to inquire.'
  • 郡大夫以盲告 - 'The prefect told him about his blindness.' '以盲告' means 'to inform about his blindness.'
  • 江以北其不盲者何限耶 - 'In the northern part of Jiang, there are countless people who are not blind.' '何限' means 'countless' or 'limitless.'
  • 于是邑令盲试之 - 'The county magistrate then tested him with blindfolds.' '盲试' means 'testing with blindfolds.'
  • 旅诸士进于郡大夫 - 'The scholars were then presented to the prefect.' '旅' means 'a group' or 'collective.'
  • 郡大夫复盲试之 - 'The prefect then tested him again with blindfolds.'
  • 旅诸士进于公 - 'The scholars were then presented to the official.' '公' refers to the official.
  • 公大奇之 - 'The official found him very remarkable.' '大奇' means 'very remarkable' or 'greatly surprised.'
  • 乃得补博士弟子员 - 'He was then allowed to join the ranks of the doctoral students.' '补' means 'to supplement' or 'to add,' and '博士弟子员' refers to the students of the Imperial College.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 嗟夫 - Interjection expressing sorrow or regret; often translated as 'Alas!' or 'Alas, indeed!'
  • 盲矣 - Literally 'is blind,' but in context, refers to someone who has lost their sight.
  • 屈其二十年锐往之气 - Literally 'bending his twenty years of sharp, forward-moving energy.' Conveys the idea of someone who has spent many years with vigor and ambition but now finds himself in a situation that diminishes his previous efforts.
  • 俯而与邑之黄口儿扶掖彳亍 - Literally 'bending down to walk with the children of the town, supporting them step by step.' Describes the act of guiding or assisting younger people, often with a sense of humility.
  • 旅进旅退 - Literally 'advancing and retreating together.' Suggests a collective movement, often in the context of following others' lead.
  • 争有司阶前盈尺地而不惭 - Literally 'struggling for a tiny space before the official's steps without shame.' Highlights the humility and lack of pride in seeking even a small place of respect.
  • 长盲矣 - Literally 'has become completely blind,' emphasizing the finality of the loss of sight.
  • 呜呜吟如往昔 - Literally 'singing softly as before,' indicating that despite blindness, the person continues to engage in activities he once enjoyed.
  • 丐其书者 - Literally 'those who beg for his writing,' referring to people who request his work.
  • 以笔濡墨纳右指窍中 - Literally 'with a brush dipped in ink, he places it in the right finger's opening.' Describes a method of writing using the mouth to hold the brush.
  • 危坐绳床 - Literally 'sitting upright on a rope bed,' describing a posture of seriousness or meditation.
  • 听他人诵，更番不令休 - Literally 'listening to others recite, taking turns without letting them rest.' Indicates a continuous and attentive engagement with others' reading.
  • 口授友人，滔滔汩汩，凡数人不能供笔札 - Literally 'dictating to friends, flowing continuously, so that several people cannot keep up with writing.' Describes the rapid and voluminous nature of his dictation.

  • 张太祝 - Refers to Zhang Taizhu, a historical figure whose specific details are less well-known but is referenced in the context of a parallel to the current situation described in the text.
  • 静原相公 - Likely refers to a high-ranking official named Jingyuan, possibly a respected figure in the author's time. The exact identity is not specified, but the title suggests a position of authority.
  • 目久不愈 - Refers to a prolonged period of blindness that does not improve, suggesting a chronic condition.

  • 进而止之欲其留，整而归之欲其洁 - '进而止之' (advancing and stopping) should leave a lingering impression ('留'), '整而归之' (concluding neatly) should be pure and clean ('洁').

  • 言未发而哀乐具乎其前 - Words not yet spoken ('言未发'), joy and sorrow are already present ('哀乐具乎其前'), describing the anticipation and emotional resonance.
  • 傥然若有见焉；其竟也，恤然若有亡焉 - As if something were seen ('傥然若有见焉'), as if something were lost ('恤然若有亡焉'), describing the fleeting and profound nature of the experience.

  • 令以数十万几累之 - Means 'ordered the tables to be piled up in tens of thousands,' describing the massive arrangement of tables.

  • 衣塑匠以深衣，巾以纶巾，方履丝绦 - Describes the attire of the '塑匠' (mason or sculptor): dressed in a deep robe, wearing a silk cap, and having square shoes tied with silk ribbons.
  • 塑匠身高六尺，广颡阔面，大有须，望之如世所绘社神者然 - Describes the physical appearance of the '塑匠' as tall, broad-shouldered, with a wide face and a full beard, resembling a village god as depicted in the world.
  • 命之升台，台高且危，塑匠怯不欲登 - Means 'ordered him to ascend the platform, which was high and precarious, causing the mason to be afraid and unwilling to climb.'
  • 献贼令军士各持弓矢，引满以向之，曰：“不登，即射！” - Means 'Xian ordered the soldiers to each hold a bow and arrow, draw them back, and point them at him, saying, 'If you do not climb, we will shoot!'' indicating the use of force to compel the mason to climb.
  • 及其半，惴慄惶惧，而万矢拟之如的，不敢止，勉登其上 - Describes the mason's fear and hesitation halfway up, with arrows aimed at him like targets, making him unable to stop and forcing him to continue climbing.
  • 献贼令三军释弓矢，罗拜其下，呼“老神仙”者三 - Means 'Xian ordered the three armies to lower their bows and arrows, surround him, and kneel down, calling out 'Old Immortal' three times,' indicating a formal worship ceremony.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 仙家有禁方而不以传世 - Literal: The Daoist masters have secret formulas but do not pass them down to the world. This phrase suggests that the Daoists possess valuable knowledge but choose not to share it with the general public.
  • 若以杀人救人为过 - Literal: If taking a life to save another is considered a fault. This phrase questions the moral implications of using secret formulas for harmful purposes.
  • 何不去此种类 - Literal: Why not eliminate this kind of person? This phrase implies that those who misuse the secret formulas should be removed from society.
  • 而止有金石草木之药乎 - Literal: And only have medicines made from minerals, stones, and herbs? This phrase contrasts the limited scope of available remedies with the potential of secret formulas.
  • 乃计不出此 - Literal: But their plan does not include this. This phrase indicates that the Daoists' strategy for dealing with misusers of secret formulas is inadequate.
  • 而往往传非其人以致遗累 - Literal: And often they pass these formulas to the wrong people, leading to trouble. This phrase highlights the consequences of improper transmission.
  • 是亦授受之未善也 - Literal: This is also a failure in the process of transmission. This phrase criticizes the lack of proper oversight in the transmission of secret formulas.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 漢史記帳中神君，不見其形，但聞其語而已 — This sentence describes a deity recorded in the *Han Shi Ji* who was only heard but not seen, emphasizing the supernatural nature of the deity.
  • 至乩仙，並其語不可得聞也，亦恍惚矣 — This sentence describes a medium or spirit communicated through a divining board, where even the words are not heard, emphasizing the elusive nature of the communication.
  • 花史嘗許予現形，一夕月明竹下，有雲鬟翠袖，倚而招予者，望之翩然；即而求之，邈然不知其所之焉 — This sentence describes a historian who promised to show his form, and a beautiful woman appeared one night, but then disappeared, leaving the speaker uncertain about what happened.
  • 是耶非耶？吾又何能測之哉 — This sentence expresses the speaker's doubt and inability to determine the truth of the experience.
  • 花史每呼予為展子 — This sentence indicates that the historian often calls the speaker by the name 'Zhan Zi.'
  • 張山來曰 — This phrase introduces a new speaker, Zhang Shanlai, who provides additional commentary.
  • 世間唯乩仙一事最為難解 — This sentence emphasizes the difficulty of understanding the practice of communicating with spirits through a divining board.
  • 或者慧業文久，死而精魂不散，偶借人間筆墨以消遣光陰耳 — This sentence suggests that the spirit of someone with long-standing wisdom and literary talent may continue to exist after death, using human tools to pass the time.
  • 古人云：‘寧為才鬼，尤勝頑仙’，則謂才鬼為仙亦無不可 — This sentence quotes an ancient saying that prefers being a talented ghost to a dull immortal, suggesting that a talented ghost can be considered a form of immortality.
  • 九牛壩觀觝戲記 — This phrase introduces the title of the text, which is a record of a wrestling match at Jiu Niu Dam.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 先王之教 - Refers to the teachings of the ancient sage kings, a common theme in Confucian and classical Chinese thought.
  • 夏仲御 - Likely refers to a historical figure or critic, possibly a contemporary or past scholar who disapproved of the subject of the text.
  • 不龟手之药 - A reference to a story in *Zhuangzi* (庄子) where a man sells a medicine to prevent cracked hands, which is later used by a statesman to save his life. This is a metaphor for hidden talent or value.
  • 鸡鸣狗盗 - A reference to two lowly skills associated with Lord Mengchang’s retinue: 'chicken-crowing' (a way to sneak into a palace) and 'dog-stealing.' This phrase is used in classical Chinese to denote unrefined or unorthodox skills that may have hidden value.
  • 浆博屠 - Refers to common laborers or people engaged in menial work, such as ferrying or butchering.
  • 千金市死马之骨 - A reference to King Zhao of Yan, who sent a thousand gold pieces to buy the bones of a dead horse in order to attract a talented horse breeder, showing his willingness to invest in talent.
  • 张元、吴昊 - Chinese names of two scholars who defected to the Western Xia during the Song dynasty, indicating the failure of Song officials to recognize and utilize talent.
  • 韩、范 - Refers to Han Qi and Fan Zhongyan, two prominent Song dynasty officials who were advocates of reform and talent management.
  • 西夏 - The Western Xia, a powerful kingdom that often opposed the Song dynasty during the 11th century.
  • 悲夫 - A classical expression of sorrow or lamentation, used to conclude the passage with a tone of despair or reflection.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 黄熟出诸番，而真腊为上 - Classical expression indicating that 'Huangshu' (a type of incense or resin) comes from various regions ('诸番'), but the best quality comes from 'Zhenla' (a historical kingdom in Southeast Asia, modern-day Cambodia).
  • 皮坚者为黄熟 - Describes the physical characteristics of high-quality 'Huangshu': tough skin or bark.
  • 桶气佳而通黑者，为夹栈黄熟 - Refers to another variety of Huangshu, distinguished by its 'bucket-like' aroma and uniformly black color.
  • 近南粤东筦茶园村土人种黄熟，如江南之艺茶 - Compares the cultivation of Huangshu by local people in the village of Chayan in Dongguan, Guangdong, to the tea-growing practices of Jiangnan (southern Jiangsu/Zhejiang).
  • 树矮枝繁，其香在根 - Describes the physical characteristics of the Huangshu tree: short and bushy, with the fragrance concentrated in the roots.
  • 自吴门解人剔根切白 - Refers to the process of preparing the root: cutting and peeling it.
  • 而香之松朽尽削，油尖铁面尽出 - Describes the result of the preparation: removing the aged, rotten parts and revealing the refined, oily, and 'iron-faced' (hard, dense) core.
  • 余与姬客半塘时 - Indicates a past event where the speaker and a woman (referred to as 'Ji') were guests at 'Bantang,' likely a place or pavilion.
  • 知金平叔最精于此，重价数购之 - States that the speaker learned from Jin Pingshu (a person) who was most skilled in processing Huangshu, and that the speaker purchased it at high prices.
  • 块者净润，长曲者如枝如虬 - Describes the physical form of the incense: some pieces are block-like and smooth, others are long and curved like branches or twisted roots.
  • 随纹缕出黄云紫绣，半杂鹧鸪斑 - Describes the visual appearance: patterns resembling yellow clouds and purple embroidery, with speckles like the plumage of the *zhuge* (a type of pheasant).
  • 可拭可玩 - Indicates that the incense is not only functional but also an object of aesthetic appreciation.
  • 寒夜小室，玉帏四垂，毾{登毛}重叠 - Describes the setting: a cold winter night in a small room, with silk curtains hanging and thick, soft rugs on the floor.
  • 烧二尺许绛蜡二三枝 - Refers to lighting a few red candles about two feet long.
  • 设参差台几，错列大小数宣炉 - Describes the setup of the room: uneven tables and various-sized incense burners arranged in a way that alternates in size.
  • 宿火常热，色如液金粟玉 - Describes the incense flame: it burns steadily, with a color like molten gold and polished jade.
  • 细拨活灰一寸，灰上隔砂选香蒸之 - Describes the precise method of incense burning: carefully raking a small amount of live ash, then placing the incense on a layer of sand to burn.
  • 历半夜，一香凝然，不焦不竭 - Describes the incense burning through the night: it burns steadily without burning out or becoming charred.
  • 郁勃氤氲，纯是糖结热香，间有梅英、半舒荷、鹅梨、蜜脾之气静参鼻观 - Describes the aroma: a dense, fragrant smoke with the scent of caramelized sweetness, interspersed with the scents of plum blossoms, half-open lotus, pear, and honeycomb.
  • 忆年来共恋此味此境，恒打晓钟，尚未着枕 - Reflects on past shared experiences of enjoying the incense and its ambiance, often staying up until the morning bell.
  • 我两人如在蕊珠众香深处 - A poetic image of the speaker and his companion being in a realm of pure fragrance and spiritual refinement.
  • 今人与香气俱散矣，安得返魂一粒，起于幽房扃室中也 - A lament that both the person and the fragrance have vanished, and a wish for a 'revival grain' to bring back life in the secluded room.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 黄熟 - Likely refers to a type of incense or aromatic resin. The term may also be linked to *huangshu* (黄熟), a traditional Chinese incense or perfume.
  • 真腊 - A historical kingdom in Southeast Asia, corresponding to modern-day Cambodia. It was known for producing high-quality incense and resins.
  • 南粤 - A classical term for the southern provinces of China, particularly Guangdong and Guangxi.
  • 东筦 - An ancient name for Dongguan, a city in Guangdong province.

  • 江南之艺茶 - Refers to the tea-growing techniques of Jiangnan (southern Jiangsu and Zhejiang), known for their refined and artistic approach to tea cultivation.
  • 吴门 - A classical term for Suzhou, a city in Jiangsu, known for its cultural and artistic sophistication.
  • 解人 - A term for a person who understands or interprets something, here likely referring to a skilled craftsman or incense preparer.
  • 剔根切白 - A process of removing the outer parts of the root and cutting the white inner core, suggesting a meticulous preparation method.
  • 松朽 - Refers to the aged, dry, or rotten parts of the root, which are removed to leave the pure, refined part.
  • 油尖铁面 - A metaphorical description of the incense’s texture: oily and hard, like iron.
  • 金平叔 - A person's name, likely a master incense maker or a figure known for his expertise in incense preparation.
  • 黄云紫绣 - A poetic description of the incense’s appearance, resembling yellow clouds and purple embroidery.
  • 鹧鸪斑 - A pattern resembling the plumage of the *zhuge* (a type of pheasant), often used in classical Chinese art and literature to describe intricate patterns.
  • 玉帏四垂 - Refers to silk curtains hanging from all sides, creating a luxurious and enclosed space.
  • 毾{登毛}重叠 - Refers to thick, soft rugs or carpets, likely made of wool or silk, layered on the floor.
  • 绛蜡 - Red candles, often used in classical Chinese settings for their aesthetic and symbolic value.
  • 参差台几 - Uneven or staggered tables, suggesting a carefully arranged and elegant setting.
  • 宣炉 - Incense burners, often made of bronze or other fine materials, used in traditional Chinese incense rituals.
  • 宿火常热 - The incense continues to burn steadily, indicating its quality and the skill of the preparation.
  • 液金粟玉 - A poetic description of the incense flame, likening its color to molten gold and polished jade.
  • 梅英 - Plum blossom.
  • 半舒荷 - Half-open lotus.
  • 鹅梨 - Goose pear (a type of fruit).
  • 蜜脾 - Honeycomb.
  • 斜倚薰笼 - A classical image from poetry, referring to a woman leaning on an incense warmer, often associated with melancholy or longing.

  • 蕊珠众香深处 - A poetic image of a place filled with the finest fragrances, often used in Daoist or Buddhist contexts to describe a realm of spiritual purity.
  • 返魂一粒 - A reference to the myth of *fanhun yilian* (返魂一粒), a legendary 'revival grain' that can bring the dead back to life, symbolizing a wish for reunion or restoration.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 璧辄忆前道人约 - 璧 refers to the protagonist, and 辄忆 means 'always remembers' or 'recalls immediately.' 前道人约 refers to a previous agreement with a Taoist monk.
  • 至鸠兹市，僦居候道人 - 至 means 'arrived at,' 鸠兹市 is the name of a market town, 僦居 means 'rented a dwelling,' and 候道人 means 'waited for the Taoist monk.'
  • 且三载 - 且 means 'and' or 'also,' and 三载 means 'three years.'
  • 一日，人大呼墙外曰 - 一日 means 'one day,' and 人大呼墙外曰 means 'someone shouted loudly outside the wall, saying.'
  • 此中匿亡者三人 - 此中 means 'here,' 匿 means 'hid,' 亡者 means 'the fugitives,' and 三人 means 'three people.'
  • 曰宋连璧、张思任、李抱真 - 曰 means 'namely,' and 宋连璧、张思任、李抱真 are the names of the three fugitives.
  • 可速出 - 可 means 'you can,' 速 means 'quickly,' and 出 means 'come out.'
  • 璧大骇无措 - 璧大骇 means '璧 was greatly startled,' and 无措 means 'at a loss.'
  • 其人已排闼入 - 其人 means 'that person,' 已 means 'already,' 排闼 means 'pushed open the door,' and 入 means 'entered.'
  • 则昔所与别道人也 - 则 means 'then,' 昔 means 'formerly,' 所与别 means 'who parted ways with,' and 道人 means 'the Taoist monk.'
  • 责之曰 - 责之 means 'reproached him,' and 曰 means 'said.'
  • 以尔夙有道契 - 以 means 'because,' 尔 means 'you,' 夙 means 'long ago,' and 有道契 means 'had a spiritual pact.'
  • 故授之书 - 故 means 'so,' and 授之书 means 'gave you the book.'
  • 尔奈何与党锢事 - 奈何 means 'why,' 与 means 'involved in,' and 党锢事 refers to the political suppression of certain groups during the Eastern Han dynasty.
  • 为天下逋逃客耶 - 为 means 'to be,' 天下 means 'the world,' 逋逃客 means 'fugitives,' and 耶 is a question particle.
  • 吾以此迟三年始至 - 吾 means 'I,' 以此 means 'for this reason,' 迟 means 'delayed,' 三年 means 'three years,' and 始至 means 'finally arrived.'
  • 璧顿首谢 - 顿首 means 'bowed deeply,' and 谢 means 'apologized.'
  • 愿自此与师永绝世缘 - 愿 means 'wishes,' 自此 means 'from now on,' 与师 means 'with the teacher,' 永绝 means 'forever sever,' and 世缘 means 'worldly ties.'
  • 不复恋妻孥矣 - 不复 means 'no longer,' 恋 means 'miss,' 妻孥 means 'wife and children,' and 矣 is a sentence-final particle.
  • 道人曰 - 道人 means 'the Taoist monk,' and 曰 means 'said.'
  • 不可 - 不可 means 'cannot.'
  • 尔还里 - 尔 means 'you,' 还 means 'return,' and 里 means 'home.'
  • 当再与家人见 - 当 means 'should,' 再 means 'again,' 与 means 'with,' 家人 means 'family,' and 见 means 'meet.'

  • 鸠兹市 - A specific market town in ancient China.
  • 党锢事 - Refers to the political suppression of certain groups during the Eastern Han dynasty, particularly the period of the 'Party Prohibitions' (黨錮).
  • 道人 - A Taoist monk or hermit.
  • 顿首 - A traditional Chinese bowing gesture used to show respect or apology.
  • 世缘 - Refers to worldly ties or connections.
  • 妻孥 - Refers to one's wife and children.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 宋連璧雖不當誤道人所期 - Complex phrase; '道人' can mean 'a Daoist' or 'a person who speaks or acts according to the Dao.' '誤道人所期' may mean 'to mislead the expectations of a Daoist' or 'to go against the expectations of a person who follows the Dao.' The full phrase suggests that although Song Lianbi did not act in accordance with what a Daoist would expect, he still had redeeming qualities.
  • 排解黨锢 - '黨锢' refers to the 'Party Suppression' or persecution of scholars and officials in the late Eastern Han dynasty. '排解' means 'to mediate' or 'to resolve.' This phrase refers to Song Lianbi's actions in mediating or resolving the persecution of these scholars.
  • 亦足見其豪侠 - '足見' means 'sufficient to show' or 'demonstrates.' '豪侠' refers to a person of chivalrous or heroic character, often associated with the 'haoxia' (豪俠) archetype in Chinese literature, a figure who acts with justice and courage, often outside the bounds of official law.
  • 虞初新志 - A classical Chinese literary anthology compiled by Zhang Huiyan in the Qing dynasty. It contains biographies and anecdotes of unusual or extraordinary individuals and events.
  • 洪都王猷定 - A Qing dynasty writer and scholar, known for his essays and historical writings. '洪都' is a reference to his place of origin or a poetic name, possibly referring to Nanchang (the capital of Jiangxi province), which was historically known as 'Hongdu.'
  • 余客会稽 - '余' is 'I,' '客' is 'to be a guest' or 'to stay as a guest,' and '会稽' is a place name, referring to the ancient city of Kuaiji, in present-day Zhejiang province.
  • 集宋公荔裳之署斋 - '宋公荔裳' is a title or honorific for Song Lichang, a scholar or official. '署斋' refers to an office or study hall, so this phrase means 'gathered at Song Lichang’s official study hall.'
  • 有客谈虎 - '有客' means 'there was a guest' or 'a visitor,' and '谈虎' is 'to talk about tigers.' This sets up the story about the tiger.
  • 属余作记 - '属' means 'to commission' or 'to request,' and '作记' means 'to write a record or account.' So this means 'he commissioned me to write a record.'
  • 高唐、孤岐诸山 - These are names of mountains, likely in the area of the county where the story takes place.
  • 穴如覆釜 - A simile. '覆釜' is a 'turned-over pot,' so the cave is shaped like an upside-down pot.
  • 三面石齿廉利 - '石齿' refers to stone teeth or jagged rocks, '廉利' means sharp or cutting. So this describes the cave's interior with sharp, jagged rocks on three sides.
  • 前壁稍平，高丈许 - '前壁' is the front wall of the cave, '稍平' is 'somewhat flat,' and '高丈许' means 'about ten feet high.' This gives a description of the cave's interior.
  • 藓落如溜 - '藓' is moss, '落' is to fall, and '如溜' means 'like a stream.' So this describes moss falling like a stream, implying a damp, flowing environment.
  • 为虎径 - '为' is 'as,' and '虎径' is 'the tiger's path.' So the moss-covered path is the tiger’s route.
  • 樵踊而蹶者数 - '樵' is a woodcutter, '踊' is to leap, '蹶' is to stumble or fall, and '数' is 'several times.' So the woodcutter leapt and stumbled several times.
  • 彷徨绕壁，泣待死 - '彷徨' is to wander uncertainly, '绕壁' is to circle the walls, '泣' is to weep, and '待死' is to await death. This describes the woodcutter's despair.
  • 日落风生，虎啸逾壁入 - '日落' is sunset, '风生' is the wind rises, '虎啸' is the tiger roars, '逾壁入' is 'to leap over the wall and enter.' This sets up the tiger's entrance.
  • 口衔生麋 - '口衔' is to carry in the mouth, '生麋' is a live deer. So the tiger brings in a live deer.
  • 分饲两小虎 - '分饲' is to feed in portions, '两小虎' is the two cubs. So the tiger feeds the cubs.
  • 见樵蹲伏，张爪奋搏 - '见樵' is to see the woodcutter, '蹲伏' is crouched, '张爪' is to stretch out claws, '奋搏' is to attack vigorously. This describes the tiger attacking the woodcutter.
  • 俄巡视若有思者 - '俄' is after a while, '巡视' is to inspect, '若有思者' is 'as if in thought.' So the tiger looks around as if thinking.
  • 反以残肉食樵 - '反' is to turn around, '残肉' is leftover meat, '食樵' is to feed the woodcutter. So the tiger feeds the woodcutter the leftover meat.
  • 入抱小虎卧 - '入抱' is to go in and embrace, '小虎卧' is the cubs lying down. So the tiger goes in and lies down with the cubs.
  • 樵私度虎饱，朝必及 - '私度' is to privately estimate, '虎饱' is the tiger is full, '朝必及' is 'will certainly come in the morning.' So the woodcutter estimates that the tiger is full and will come again in the morning.
  • 昧爽，虎跃而出 - '昧爽' is the early morning, '虎跃而出' is the tiger jumps out. This describes the tiger’s departure.
  • 停午，复衔一麂来 - '停午' is noon, '复' is again, '衔' is to carry, '一麂' is a young deer. So the tiger returns at noon with another young deer.
  • 饲其子，仍投餕与樵 - '饲其子' is to feed its cubs, '仍投餕' is to still give the scraps, '与樵' is to the woodcutter. So the tiger feeds its cubs and gives the scraps to the woodcutter.
  • 樵馁甚，取啖，渴自饮其溺 - '馁甚' is very hungry, '取啖' is to take and eat, '渴自饮其溺' is 'thirsty, he drinks the tiger’s urine.' This shows the woodcutter’s desperation.
  • 如是者弥月，浸与虎狎 - '如是' is like this, '弥月' is a full month, '浸与虎狎' is 'gradually becoming familiar with the tiger.' So this describes the month-long period in which the woodcutter becomes familiar with the tiger.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 宋连璧 - A historical or literary figure, possibly a scholar or official from the Song dynasty. The name may be a reference to a person known for his integrity or actions in the context of political suppression ('党锢').
  • 党锢 - A historical event in the late Eastern Han dynasty, when the imperial court, under the influence of powerful eunuchs, persecuted scholars and officials who were associated with reformist or moralist groups. This was a significant event in Chinese history and is referenced here as a demonstration of Song Lianbi’s character.
  • 虞初新志 - A literary anthology from the Qing dynasty, edited by Zhang Huiyan. It contains biographies and anecdotes of unusual people and events, often with a focus on moral or heroic figures.
  • 洪都王猷定 - A Qing dynasty scholar and writer, known for his essays and historical works. The term '洪都' may refer to Nanchang.

Proper Nouns

  • 辛丑春 - A date in the Chinese sexagenary cycle. '辛丑' is a combination of heavenly stems and earthly branches, which was used in traditional Chinese calendrical systems. '辛丑春' would mean 'spring of the Xin Chou year.'
  • 于一文津选本 - This is likely the title of a selection or compilation of Wanyu’s works, possibly by a scholar named 'Yi Wenjin.' However, this is uncertain and may be a misreading or a name that is not widely known in classical Chinese literature.
  • 于一文 - Possibly the name of a scholar or compiler, though uncertain.
  • 王猷定 - A Qing dynasty scholar and writer, known for his essays and historical works.

  • 須臾 - Means 'in a moment' or 'soon,' indicating a short period of time.
  • 拳雙足俛首就樵 - Means 'the tiger knelt on its forepaws, lowered its head, and approached the woodcutter,' where '拳雙足' means 'kneeling on its forepaws' and '俛首' means 'lowering its head.'
  • 樵騎虎，騰壁上 - Means 'the woodcutter rode the tiger, leaping onto the wall,' where '騰壁上' means 'leaping onto the wall.'
  • 虎置樵，携子行 - Means 'the tiger set down the woodcutter and carried the cub away,' where '置樵' means 'set down the woodcutter' and '携子行' means 'carried the cub away.'
  • 陰崖灌莽 - Means 'a dark cliff with dense vegetation,' where '陰崖' means 'dark cliff' and '灌莽' means 'dense vegetation.'
  • 禽鳥聲絕 - Means 'the sounds of birds were silent,' indicating a desolate environment.
  • 風猎猎从黑林生 - Means 'the wind whistled through the dark forest,' where '猎猎' describes the sound of the wind.
  • 樵益急 - Means 'the woodcutter became even more urgent,' where '益急' means 'became even more urgent.'
  • 蒙大王活我 - Means 'I was saved by Your Majesty,' where '蒙' means 'to be favored by' and '活我' means 'saved me.'
  • 相失 - Means 'to lose each other,' indicating separation.
  • 惧不免他患 - Means 'fear of falling into other misfortunes,' where '惧' means 'fear' and '不免他患' means 'not escaping other misfortunes.'
  • 幸终活我 - Means 'may you keep me alive until the end,' where '幸' means 'may' and '终活我' means 'keep me alive until the end.'
  • 导我中衢 - Means 'guide me to the main road,' where '导' means 'guide' and '中衢' means 'main road.'
  • 归当畜一豚 - Means 'when I return, I will raise a pig,' where '归' means 'return' and '畜一豚' means 'raise a pig.'

  • 某日时过飨 - Means 'on a certain day and time, I will offer you a feast,' where '某日时' means 'a certain day and time' and '过飨' means 'offer a feast.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 樵 - Refers to a woodcutter, a common figure in classical literature often symbolizing simplicity and hardship.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 谪居 - Term meaning 'exile' or 'banishment,' often used in the context of officials being demoted or sent to remote areas.
  • 崎岖三千里 - Literal meaning 'three thousand miles of rugged terrain,' used to describe a long and difficult journey.
  • 邮亭驿壁 - Refers to the walls of post stations or inns along the way, where travelers often left inscriptions or poems.
  • 迁客 - Refers to officials who have been exiled or demoted.
  • 赐环诏 - Metaphorical term referring to an imperial decree that allows exiled officials to return home, often symbolizing forgiveness or restoration.
  • 上圣明 - Term of praise for the emperor, meaning 'the most wise and enlightened ruler.'
  • 汤沐邑 - Refers to a fief or domain granted to officials, often as a reward or honor.
  • 出关迁客皆才子 - Statement suggesting that all exiled officials are talented individuals.
  • 不患无友 - Phrase meaning 'there is no fear of lacking friends,' implying that the exile will still have companions.
  • 粮尽，馁而啼 - Describes the state of starvation and distress, with '馁' meaning 'hungry' and '啼' meaning 'to cry.'
  • 孺子妾慰劳 - Refers to a concubine or minor wife offering comfort and support.
  • 卿有友，必箪食迎若 - Promise that if the exiled official has friends, they will welcome him with food, using '箪食' (a basket of food) as a symbol of hospitality.
  • 恐如卿言，当先以酒疗吾渴 - Response indicating that the exiled official hopes to be greeted with alcohol to quench his thirst.
  • 卜筑东冈 - Means 'to build a dwelling on the eastern hill,' indicating the establishment of a new home.
  • 躬自饭牛 - Describes the act of personally feeding cattle, emphasizing humility and manual labor.
  • 牧竖 - Refers to young herdsmen.
  • 温厚 - Means 'warm and thick' in the context of poetry, describing a style that is gentle and profound.
  • 迁谪态 - Refers to the demeanor or attitude of someone who has been exiled or demoted.
  • 国子藩公 - Refers to a high-ranking official, likely a member of the imperial court.
  • 西园飞盖 - Describes a grand procession or gathering, often associated with the elite or nobility.
  • 礼为上客 - Means 'treated as a guest of honor,' indicating high respect and status.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 谪居东，崎岖三千里 - Evokes the theme of exile and the hardships faced by officials who were sent to remote areas.
  • 邮亭驿壁，读迁客诗 - Refers to the tradition of leaving poetry at post stations or inns, a practice that was common among exiled officials.

  • 西园飞盖 - Describes a grand procession or gathering, often associated with the elite or nobility, reflecting the social and political life of the time.

  • 西园 - The name of a place or garden, often associated with grand gatherings and elite social life.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 然药园亦困甚 - 然 means 'but' or 'however,' and 困甚 means 'greatly distressed' or 'extremely poor.' This phrase indicates a contrast to the previous situation.
  • 塞上风刺入骨 - A vivid description of the cold wind on the frontier, literally 'the wind on the frontier pierces to the bone.'
  • 秋即雨雪 - 即 means 'then' or 'soon after,' indicating that rain and snow follow the onset of autumn.
  • 山川林木尽白 - A poetic way of saying that the landscape turns completely white with snow.
  • 河冰合，常不得汲 - 河冰合 means 'the river freezes,' and 常不得汲 means 'often unable to draw water.'
  • 樵苏不至，五日不爨 - 樵苏 refers to firewood, and 爨 means 'to cook.' This phrase indicates a severe shortage of firewood, leading to no cooking for five days.
  • 取芦粟小米，和雪啮之 - 芦粟小米 refers to millet, and 啮之 means 'chew it.' This describes the desperate act of eating millet mixed with snow.
  • 然孺子妾辄生子 - 孺子妾 refers to young wives, and 辄生子 means 'give birth to children.' This indicates that despite the harsh conditions, the young wives continue to bear children.
  • 当尔时，坐茅屋下，日照户，如渥醇酒 - 当尔时 means 'at that time,' 坐茅屋下 means 'sit under a thatched house,' 日照户 means 'sunlight shines through the door,' and 如渥醇酒 means 'like being bathed in fine wine.' This describes a moment of relative comfort.
  • 然畏风不能视日 - 畏风 means 'fear the wind,' and 不能视日 means 'cannot look at the sun.' This indicates that the cold wind makes it difficult to enjoy the sunlight.
  • 日哺，山鬼夜啼，饥鼯声咽 - 日哺 means 'late afternoon,' 山鬼夜啼 refers to the eerie sounds of the night, and 饥鼯声咽 describes the sounds of hungry weasels. This sets a somber and eerie atmosphere.
  • 忽闻叩门客，翩然有喜 - 忽闻 means 'suddenly hear,' 叩门客 means 'a visitor knocking at the door,' and 翩然有喜 means 'elegantly and happily.' This describes a sudden and pleasant surprise.
  • 从隙中窥之，虎方以尾击户 - 从隙中窥之 means 'look through a crack,' 虎方以尾击户 means 'a tiger is hitting the door with its tail.' This indicates a dangerous situation.
  • 药园危坐自若 - 危坐 means 'sit upright,' and 自若 means 'calm and composed.' This describes the protagonist's calm demeanor despite the danger.
  • 居东凡五迁，家日贫，诗日富 - 居东 means 'live in the east,' 凡五迁 means 'move five times,' 家日贫 means 'the family becomes poorer day by day,' and 诗日富 means 'poetry becomes richer day by day.' This describes the protagonist's life in the east.
  • 登临眺览，供其笔墨，作《归思轩记》以寓意 - 登临眺览 means 'climb and look around,' 供其笔墨 means 'provide him with writing materials,' and 作《归思轩记》以寓意 means 'write the 'Return Thoughts Pavilion Record' to convey his thoughts.' This describes the protagonist's creative activity.
  • 友人林璐闻之，曰：“卿归矣！曩者邯郸道上吕仙祠，即卢生受枕处也。仕宦过者，疾驱去以避不祥。卿衔命过其下，停车徐步入。道人方坐蒲团不起。卿异之，索笔题壁曰：『向翁乞取还乡梦，留得凌云化鹤飞』之句，得非诗谶耶？”贻书报药园，惘然悟 - 友人林璐闻之 means 'a friend named Lin Lu heard about it,' 卿归矣 means 'you are returning,' 曩者邯郸道上吕仙祠 refers to the Lü Xian Temple on the road to Handan, 即卢生受枕处也 means 'where Lu Sheng received the magic pillow,' 仕宦过者，疾驱去以避不祥 means 'officials passing by hurry away to avoid misfortune,' 卿衔命过其下，停车徐步入 means 'you received an order to pass by, stopped the carriage, and slowly walked in,' 道人方坐蒲团不起 means 'the monk sat on a mat and did not get up,' 卿异之，索笔题壁曰 means 'you were surprised and asked for a pen to write on the wall,' 『向翁乞取还乡梦，留得凌云化鹤飞』之句 means 'the words 'I ask the old man for a dream of returning home, leaving behind the clouds and flying like a crane,' 得非诗谶耶 means 'is this not a poetic omen?' 贻书报药园，惘然悟 means 'Lin Lu sent a letter to Yeyuan, who was confused but understood.'
  • 又一年始归，果如林生言 - 又一年始归 means 'after another year, you returned,' 果如林生言 means 'as Lin Lu predicted.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 塞上 - Refers to the frontier regions, often associated with hardship and isolation.
  • 吕仙祠 - The Lü Xian Temple, dedicated to Lü Dongbin, a legendary figure in Chinese Taoism.
  • 卢生受枕处 - Refers to the story of Lu Sheng, who had a magical dream of a luxurious life after receiving a magic pillow.
  • 仕宦过者，疾驱去以避不祥 - A reference to the superstition that officials avoid passing by the Lü Xian Temple to avoid misfortune.
  • 邯郸道 - The road to Handan, a city in Hebei Province, known for the story of Lu Sheng.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 然药园亦困甚，塞上风刺入骨，秋即雨雪，山川林木尽白，河冰合，常不得汲 - This long sequence describes the harsh conditions in the frontier, with each clause building on the previous one.
  • 樵苏不至，五日不爨，取芦粟小米，和雪啮之 - This sequence describes the extreme poverty and lack of resources.
  • 然孺子妾辄生子 - This clause contrasts the harsh conditions with the continuation of life.
  • 当尔时，坐茅屋下，日照户，如渥醇酒，然畏风不能视日 - This sequence describes a moment of relative comfort, followed by the discomfort caused by the wind.
  • 日哺，山鬼夜啼，饥鼯声咽，忽闻叩门客，翩然有喜 - This sequence sets a somber and eerie atmosphere, followed by a sudden and pleasant surprise.
  • 从隙中窥之，虎方以尾击户 - This clause describes a dangerous situation.
  • 药园危坐自若 - This clause describes the protagonist's calm demeanor despite the danger.
  • 居东凡五迁，家日贫，诗日富 - This sequence describes the protagonist's life in the east, with the family becoming poorer and the poetry becoming richer.
  • 登临眺览，供其笔墨，作《归思轩记》以寓意 - This sequence describes the protagonist's creative activity.
  • 友人林璐闻之，曰：“卿归矣！曩者邯郸道上吕仙祠，即卢生受枕处也。仕宦过者，疾驱去以避不祥。卿衔命过其下，停车徐步入。道人方坐蒲团不起。卿异之，索笔题壁曰：『向翁乞取还乡梦，留得凌云化鹤飞』之句，得非诗谶耶？”贻书报药园，惘然悟 - This long sequence describes a friend's interpretation of the protagonist's actions and the subsequent realization.
  • 又一年始归，果如林生言 - This clause describes the protagonist's return, as predicted by the friend.

Proper Nouns

  • 药园 - The protagonist's name or nickname.
  • 林璐 - The name of the friend who interprets the protagonist's actions.
