Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The opening sentence describes Xiaolian's exceptional qualities and her impact on the capital's people. It can be read as a single, cohesive statement.
  • This sentence describes the elaborate process required to secure Xiaolian's presence at a banquet. The structure is complex but clear.
  • This sentence contrasts the guests' expectations with Xiaolian's actual behavior. The contrast is clear but requires careful translation to maintain the nuance.
  • This sentence introduces Fan Xinghua and his residence in Yan. The structure is straightforward but requires clarity in translation.
  • This sentence describes Fan's initial reaction to Xiaolian. The contrast between his initial impression and his subsequent behavior is key.
  • This sentence describes Xiaolian's focused attention on Fan throughout the banquet. The repetition of '目' (mù) emphasizes her intense focus.
  • These sentences continue the pattern of Xiaolian's focused attention on Fan. The repetition of '范' (fàn) and '视' (shì) emphasizes the continuous nature of her attention.
  • This sentence describes Xiaolian's behavior as Fan leaves and returns. The repetition of '目' (mù) emphasizes her continuous attention.
  • This sentence describes Xiaolian's behavior as she stands up and walks away. The repetition of '必回头视范' (bì huítóu shì fàn) emphasizes her intense focus.
  • This phrase describes Xiaolian's fear of losing sight of Fan. The metaphorical use of '失之' (shī zhī) adds depth to her behavior.
  • This sentence contrasts Xiaolian's usual reserved nature with her sudden behavior. The contrast is key to understanding her character.
  • This sentence describes the reaction of the people at the table. The collective astonishment is important to convey.
  • This sentence describes Fan's discomfort and unease. The use of '跼蹐' (jújí) emphasizes his feelings.
  • This sentence describes Xiaolian's composed demeanor. The use of '自如' (zìrú) emphasizes her calmness.
  • This sentence describes Xiaolian's detailed inquiry about Fan's name. The use of '详问' (xiángwèn) emphasizes her thoroughness.
  • This sentence describes Xiaolian's recitation of Fan's name. The use of '朝夕' (zhāoxī) emphasizes the frequency of her recitation.

  • 命生坐 - '命' means 'to order,' and '生' refers to the qin player.
  • 怒目視 - Means 'to glare at.'
  • 博通万卷书 - Means 'well-versed in ten thousand volumes of books.'
  • 唯知 - Means 'only know.'
  • 马上用剑槊 - Refers to military skills, meaning 'to use a sword and spear on horseback.'
  • 吾岂为若门下士耶 - Means 'Am I to be your subordinate?'
  • 奈何不以宾礼见而屈于旁 - Means 'Why are you not treating me with the courtesy due to a guest and seating me on the side?'
  • 奮衣徑出 - '奮衣' means 'to throw off one's clothes,' and '徑出' means 'to leave directly.'
  • 下與抗禮謝罪 - '下與抗禮' means 'to treat with equal respect,' and '謝罪' means 'to apologize.'
  • 强留之 - Means 'forced him to stay.'
  • 踞上坐 - Means 'sat in the upper seat.'
  • 称善 - Means 'praised him.'
  • 竦听 - Means 'listened in awe.'
  • 枝上无螳螂捕蝉 - Metaphor for peace, meaning 'there are no cicadas being caught by mantises.'
  • 西北肃杀声 - Refers to a harsh, desolate sound associated with the northwest.
  • 琴者，天下之至和也 - Means 'The qin is the most harmonious instrument in the world.'
  • 雝雝如鸾凤鸣 - '雝雝' means 'harmonious,' and '鸾凤鸣' means 'the call of phoenixes.'
  • 枝上无螳螂捕蝉，而弦中忽变西北肃杀声 - Metaphor for peace and the sudden change in the qin's sound to a harsh, desolate tone.
  • 岂军中殆将有警耶 - Means 'Is there perhaps a warning in the army?'
  • 三军之士皆为嗟叹 - Means 'All the soldiers sighed.'
  • 流涕 - Means 'shed tears.'
  • 尽醉 - Means 'completely drunk.'
  • 痛哭 - Means 'wept bitterly.'
  • 上马而去 - Means 'mounted his horse and left.'
  • 后此军沦于海澄焉 - '沦' means 'defeated,' and '海澄' refers to Haicheng.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 惠州 - Refers to Huizhou, a city in Guangdong Province.
  • 僧上振 - Refers to a monk named Shang Zhen.
  • 八闽 - Refers to the eight provinces of Fujian.
  • 王公大人 - Refers to princes and high officials.
  • 满洲 - Refers to Manchuria, the region from which the Qing Dynasty rulers originated.
  • 闽省 - Refers to Fujian Province.
  • 剑槊 - Refers to a sword and spear, traditional weapons used in Chinese warfare.
  • 宾礼 - Refers to the formal etiquette used when receiving guests.
  • 秦音 - Refers to the musical style of the Qin state, known for its harsh and mournful tones.
  • 三军 - Refers to the three branches of the military (infantry, cavalry, and chariots).
  • 海澄 - Refers to Haicheng, a place where the army was defeated.

[145]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 久之 - Temporal expression meaning 'after a long time' or 'eventually,' indicating a passage of time.
  • 目生为琴师 - Figurative expression meaning 'the people regarded him as a qin player.' The verb '目' (mù) here means 'to regard' or 'to consider.'
  • 虽江浙间，颇多闻其名者 - Concessive phrase meaning 'even in Jiang-Zhe, many had heard of his name.' The word '虽' (suī) introduces a concession.
  • 然当道不以礼遇 - Contrastive phrase meaning 'however, those in power did not treat him with courtesy.' The word '然' (rán) introduces a contrast.
  • 招亦不往，往亦不为久留 - Parallel construction meaning 'he would not go when invited, and if he did go, he would not stay long.' The repetition emphasizes his reluctance to engage with authority.
  • 常酒后耳热 - Colloquial expression meaning 'he often got heated after drinking,' describing a state of emotional or physical warmth after alcohol.
  • 摔琴于地 - Vivid action meaning 'he would throw his qin to the ground,' symbolizing frustration or emotional release.
  • 引满大卮 - Gesture meaning 'he would pour himself a large cup,' indicating indulgence in drink.
  • 放言高论 - Phrase meaning 'he would speak freely and loftily,' describing unrestrained and elevated discourse.
  • 惊其座宾 - Resultative phrase meaning 'he would startle the guests at the table,' indicating the impact of his behavior.
  • 谈古今得失 - Broad topic meaning 'he would discuss the gains and losses of past and present,' covering a wide range of historical and contemporary issues.
  • 虽老师宿儒，深通经济者，不能难也 - Concessive and evaluative phrase meaning 'even scholars and teachers well-versed in statecraft could not challenge him,' emphasizing his intellectual prowess.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 闽人 - Refers to people from Fujian, a region in southeastern China.
  • 琴师 - Refers to a qin player, where the qin is a traditional Chinese zither, often associated with scholarly and refined culture.
  • 江浙 - Refers to Jiangsu and Zhejiang provinces, historically known for their cultural and economic significance.
  • 当道 - Refers to those in power or authority, often used in a neutral or slightly derogatory sense.
  • 礼遇 - Refers to courteous treatment, a key concept in Confucian etiquette.
  • 老师宿儒 - Refers to experienced and respected scholars, often associated with Confucian learning.
  • 经济 - Refers to statecraft or governance, a broad term encompassing political and administrative knowledge.

Proper Nouns

  • 闽人 - People from Fujian.
  • 琴师 - Qin player.
  • 江浙 - Jiangsu and Zhejiang provinces.
  • 当道 - Those in power.
  • 老师宿儒 - Experienced scholars.
  • 经济 - Statecraft.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 其最爱童子曰金兰 - His favorite young man was called Jinlan. Jinlan may have symbolic or cultural significance.
  • 独得生传 - He alone received his transmission. Suggests a special relationship or inheritance of knowledge.
  • 常负奚囊从生游数千里外 - He often carried a small bag and traveled thousands of miles with him. '奚囊' refers to a small bag, often used by scholars or travelers.
  • 生诗成，金兰辄缮录之盈帙 - When the master's poems were completed, Jinlan would always copy them into volumes. '缮录' means to carefully copy, and '盈帙' means to fill volumes.
  • 客访生不遇，金兰代为款接，以生惊人句示人 - When guests visited the master and did not find him, Jinlan would entertain them and show them the master's astonishing lines. '款接' means to entertain or receive hospitably.
  • 以为抱负非常之士，不得志而隐于琴 - They thought he was a man of extraordinary talent who, despite his ambitions, had not achieved success and thus retreated into music. '抱负' means ambition or aspiration, and '不得志' means unfulfilled ambitions.
  • 然当事卒莫有荐之者，竟佯狂以卒云 - However, in the end, no one recommended him, and he died pretending to be mad. '佯狂' means to pretend to be mad or insane.
  • 生笃于伉俪 - The master was devoted to his wife. '笃于伉俪' means to be deeply devoted to one's spouse.
  • 妇陈氏，少生十岁，亦颇知书嗜音 - His wife, Chen, was ten years younger than him and also quite knowledgeable in books and fond of music. '颇知书' means quite knowledgeable in books, and '嗜音' means fond of music.
  • 生尝入为其妻鼓琴，茶香入牖，鬓影萧疏，顾而乐之，以为闺房清课，亦人生韵事 - The master once played the qin for his wife. The fragrance of tea wafted through the window, and his sparse hair reflected in the light. He looked around and found joy in it, thinking that playing the qin in the boudoir was also a refined and elegant affair. '顾而乐之' means to look around and find joy in it, and '闺房清课' means a refined activity in the boudoir.
  • 忽一日谓其妇曰：“吾向闻红颜薄命。卿才情如此，而推命者多言岁行在卯当死。岂汝亦天上人，不久当去耶？” - One day, he told his wife, 'I have heard that beautiful women have short lives. With your talent and emotions, many fortune-tellers say you will die when the year is in the year of the rabbit. Are you also a celestial being, about to depart soon?' '红颜薄命' means beautiful women have short lives, and '岁行在卯' means the year is in the year of the rabbit.
  • 因感慨悲伤，为弹《别鹄离鸾》之曲，曰：“琴音和，吾与汝尚无恙，然第七弦无故忽绝，少而慧者当之。” - He then felt sad and played the 'Farewell to the Swan and the Phoenix' tune, saying, 'The qin sounds harmonious, and we are still well, but the seventh string suddenly broke without reason. It seems the young and wise one will be affected.' '别鹄离鸾' means farewell to the swan and the phoenix, and '第七弦无故忽绝' means the seventh string suddenly broke without reason.
  • 居数日，金兰死 - A few days later, Jinlan died. This is a straightforward statement.
  • 生抚尸一哭，不胜其悲，吐血数斗，曰：“吾死后，《广陵散》绝矣。”遂焚其琴，不复鼓也。因自号“焚琴子” - The master cried over the body, unable to bear the sorrow, and spat out several dou of blood, saying, 'After my death, the 'Guangling San' will be lost.' He then burned his qin and stopped playing it. He then called himself 'Fen Qinzi.' '广陵散' is a famous qin piece, and '焚琴子' means the man who burned his qin.
  • 生至康熙丁巳，年四十九，竟卒。闻其妇先亡一岁云 - The master died in the year Ding Si of the Kangxi era, at the age of forty-nine. It is said that his wife had died a year earlier. '康熙丁巳' refers to the year Ding Si in the Kangxi era, and '竟卒' means he finally died.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 金兰 - Jinlan is likely a symbolic name, possibly referring to a close friend or a disciple. In Chinese culture, '金兰' (jinlan) often symbolizes a deep and loyal friendship.
  • 《别鹄离鸾》 - 'Farewell to the Swan and the Phoenix' is a traditional Chinese qin piece that expresses the sorrow of separation. It is often associated with the story of the swan and the phoenix, which symbolize a harmonious and virtuous couple.
  • 《广陵散》 - 'Guangling San' is one of the most famous qin pieces in Chinese history. It is known for its complex and powerful melodies and is often associated with the story of the musician Ruan Ji, who composed it in response to political turmoil.
  • 康熙丁巳 - The year Ding Si in the Kangxi era corresponds to 1687. The Kangxi era (1662-1722) was a period of significant cultural and economic development in China.
  • 红颜薄命 - This phrase is a common saying in Chinese literature, referring to the idea that beautiful women often have short and tragic lives. It is often used in classical literature to express sympathy for women who suffer.
  • 岁行在卯 - This refers to the Chinese zodiac, where the year of the rabbit (卯) is one of the twelve animals. In Chinese astrology, the year of the rabbit is often associated with specific characteristics and predictions.
  • 焚琴子 - This is a self-proclaimed title that the master adopted after burning his qin. It symbolizes his deep sorrow and the loss of his musical talent.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 顧子曰 - Classical introductory phrase meaning 'Gongzi said'; '顧子' is a respectful way to address the author or a figure of authority.
  • 焚琴子之事 - Refers to the story of 'Burning the Zither,' a metaphorical or literal act of destroying a musical instrument, often symbolizing the loss of one's passion or talent.
  • 余盖闻之漳州陈别驾云 - A formal way of saying 'I have heard from the Deputy Magistrate of Zhangzhou,' where '盖' indicates hearsay or indirect knowledge.
  • 别驾为余言最详 - Means 'the Deputy Magistrate spoke to me in great detail,' emphasizing the thoroughness of the account.
  • 因嘱余亟为立传 - Means 'and so he urged me to quickly write a biography,' where '亟' conveys urgency.
  • 殆古之有心人也 - Means 'he is indeed a person of great concern,' where '殆' suggests a strong likelihood or certainty.
  • 观生之少而肆于文 - Means 'Observing that he was young and indulged in writing,' where '肆' implies unrestrained or excessive behavior.
  • 文不得志而游 - Means 'his writing did not achieve success, so he wandered,' where '不得志' suggests unfulfilled aspirations.
  • 一寄于琴，再寄于哭 - Means 'he first found solace in the zither, then in weeping,' where '寄' means to rely on or寄托 (rely on).
  • 卒之无有识生之才而用之者 - Means 'in the end, there was no one who recognized his talent and put it to use,' where '卒之' indicates the final outcome.
  • 宜其伤于情而碎于琴也 - Means 'it was fitting that he was hurt by his emotions and destroyed his zither,' where '宜' suggests that the outcome was appropriate or inevitable.
  • 然生流风余韵，宛在丹山碧水之间 - Means 'yet his lingering influence and graceful demeanor seem to remain in the red mountains and blue waters,' where '流风余韵' refers to his lasting legacy.
  • 迄今登鼓山之亭，如闻其哭焉 - Means 'even now, when one ascends the pavilion on Drum Mountain, one can still hear his weeping,' where '迄今' indicates 'even to this day.'
  • 生其化鹤而来归乎？ - Means 'Has he perhaps transformed into a crane and returned?' where '化鹤' is a metaphor for immortality or spiritual transformation.
  • 松风夜弦，空林鬼哭，生何往而不在也？ - Means 'The pine wind plays the night strings, and the ghosts weep in the empty forest—wherever he goes, he is always present,' where '夜弦' refers to the zither being played at night.
  • 悲哉！ - A simple expression of sorrow or lamentation.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 焚琴子 - A metaphorical or literal reference to someone who destroys their zither, symbolizing the loss of passion or talent.
  • 漳州陈别驾 - Refers to a Deputy Magistrate of Zhangzhou, a historical figure or official who provided the account.
  • 鼓山 - Refers to Drum Mountain, a specific location in Chinese history or literature, often associated with scenic beauty or cultural significance.
  • 化鹤 - A metaphor for immortality or spiritual transformation, often associated with Taoist or Buddhist beliefs.
  • 松风夜弦 - A poetic image of the zither being played under the pine wind at night, evoking a sense of melancholy or transcendence.

Proper Nouns

  • 顧子 - A respectful address, likely referring to the author or a figure of authority.
  • 焚琴子 - A metaphorical or literal reference to someone who destroys their zither.
  • 漳州陈别驾 - A specific historical figure or official from Zhangzhou.
  • 鼓山 - A specific location, likely associated with cultural or historical significance.
  • 化鹤 - A metaphor for immortality or spiritual transformation, often associated with Taoist or Buddhist beliefs.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 四氏子 - A nickname given to a man from the Wu region during the early Ming Dynasty, reflecting his family background or personality.
  • 萬歷初 - Refers to the early years of the Wanli reign period (1573–1620) of the Ming Dynasty.
  • 有姓名，四氏子者，人名之，因以為名焉 - Indicates that the man was given the nickname '四氏子' by others, and he adopted it as his own name.
  • 氏子家雖貧，亦產清門 - '氏子家' refers to the family of the man named '四氏子.' '產清門' means 'of a pure and noble lineage,' suggesting that despite being poor, the family has a good reputation.
  • 凡缨緌之徒，初皆与游 - '缨緌之徒' refers to scholars or literati, and '初皆与游' means 'initially they all associated with him.'
  • 痴黠各半，亦复各时 - '痴黠各半' means 'half foolish and half clever,' and '亦复各时' means 'also varies with the time,' indicating that the man's behavior changes depending on the situation.
  • 方其黠也，能作诗文，自作自书自讽，声满四邻，若出金石 - '方其黠也' means 'when he is clever,' '能作诗文' means 'can write poetry and prose,' '自作自书自讽' means 'write, copy, and recite his own works,' and '声满四邻，若出金石' means 'his voice fills the neighborhood, as if it comes from bells and drums.'
  • 及其痴也，天地变，黑白贸，亲疏怨德皆相反，妻孥无协志者 - '及其痴也' means 'when he is foolish,' '天地变，黑白贸' means 'the sky and earth change, black and white are reversed,' '亲疏怨德皆相反' means 'the relationships of closeness and distance, resentment and virtue are all reversed,' and '妻孥无协志者' means 'his wife and children have no common goals.'
  • 其父痛谕之，不从，则挝之，氏子亦报挝焉 - '其父痛谕之' means 'his father sternly advised him,' '不从' means 'he did not listen,' '则挝之' means 'then beat him,' and '氏子亦报挝焉' means 'the man also retaliated by beating his father.'
  • 久之，恒挝其父 - '久之' means 'after a long time,' and '恒挝其父' means 'he constantly beat his father.'
  • 既而著为论曰 - '既而' means 'then,' and '著为论曰' means 'wrote an essay saying.'
  • 父子主亲，父若挞子，当其举手之时，亲谊已绝，子安得不报挞 - '父子主亲' means 'the father and son are bound by kinship,' '父若挞子' means 'if the father beats the son,' '当其举手之时' means 'at the moment he raises his hand,' '亲谊已绝' means 'the bond of kinship is severed,' and '子安得不报挞' means 'how can the son not retaliate?'
  • 又且君父一也，君有罪，汤武诛之，可以称圣；父有罪，子挞之，容得不号贤乎 - '又且君父一也' means 'and the ruler and father are one,' '君有罪，汤武诛之，可以称圣' means 'if the ruler commits a crime, Tang and Wu can punish him and be called sage kings,' and '父有罪，子挞之，容得不号贤乎' means 'if the father commits a crime, the son can beat him and be called virtuous.'
  • 又立论 - '又立论' means 'he also wrote another essay.'
  • 古今无真名人，但能诃诋人则名归之 - '古今无真名人' means 'there are no truly famous people in ancient and modern times,' and '但能诃诋人则名归之' means 'only those who can criticize others gain fame.'
  • 孟子诋杨、墨，庄周诋孔子，韩愈诋佛，岂好诋人哉？自为名焉耳 - '孟子诋杨、墨' means 'Mengzi criticized Yang and Mo,' '庄周诋孔子' means 'Zhuangzi criticized Confucius,' '韩愈诋佛' means 'Han Yu criticized Buddhism,' and '岂好诋人哉？自为名焉耳' means 'how could they enjoy criticizing others? They did it for their own fame.'
  • 故氏子遇当世大儒，其声名经旸谷、达濛汜者，皆极力訾诟之 - '故氏子遇当世大儒' means 'therefore, the man encountered the great scholars of his time,' '其声名经旸谷、达濛汜者' means 'those whose fame reached the eastern and western extremes,' and '皆极力訾诟之' means 'he criticized them fiercely.'
  • 且作嗔拳笑面曰 - '且作嗔拳笑面曰' means 'he said with a scowling face and a smiling mouth.'
  • 是才不如我，而名居吾上，何也 - '是才不如我' means 'his talent is not as good as mine,' '而名居吾上' means 'but his fame is above mine,' and '何也' means 'why is that?'
  • 或相见至有受其大诟者 - '或相见至有受其大诟者' means 'sometimes when they met, they were severely criticized by him.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 萬歷初 - The early years of the Wanli reign period (1573–1620) of the Ming Dynasty.
  • 四氏子 - A nickname given to a man from the Wu region during the early Ming Dynasty.
  • 孟子 - Mencius, a Confucian philosopher.
  • 杨、墨 - Yang Zhu and Mozi, philosophers who were criticized by Mencius.
  • 庄周 - Zhuangzi, a Daoist philosopher.
  • 韩愈 - Han Yu, a Tang Dynasty Confucian scholar and critic of Buddhism.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 四氏子，萬歷初吳人也。有姓名，四氏子者，人名之，因以為名焉。
  • 氏子家雖貧，亦產清門，凡缨緌之徒，初皆与游。
  • 痴黠各半，亦复各时。
  • 方其黠也，能作诗文，自作自书自讽，声满四邻，若出金石。
  • 及其痴也，天地变，黑白贸，亲疏怨德皆相反，妻孥无协志者。
  • 其父痛谕之，不从，则挝之，氏子亦报挝焉。
  • 久之，恒挝其父。
  • 既而著为论曰。
  • 父子主亲，父若挞子，当其举手之时，亲谊已绝，子安得不报挞。
  • 又且君父一也，君有罪，汤武诛之，可以称圣；父有罪，子挞之，容得不号贤乎。
  • 又立论。
  • 古今无真名人，但能诃诋人则名归之。
  • 孟子诋杨、墨，庄周诋孔子，韩愈诋佛，岂好诋人哉？自为名焉耳。
  • 故氏子遇当世大儒，其声名经旸谷、达濛汜者，皆极力訾诟之。
  • 且作嗔拳笑面曰。
  • 是才不如我，而名居吾上，何也。
  • 或相见至有受其大诟者。

[149]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 氏子 - Refers to a son of a certain family, used here to denote a specific individual.
  • 挝父母 - Means to strike or beat one's parents, indicating extreme disrespect.
  • 詈兄嫂 - Means to curse or insult one's elder brother and sister-in-law.
  • 诋諆当世之岳立者 - Means to slander or criticize those who are respected and influential in the present time.
  • 国人皆鄙之 - Means that the people all looked down on him, indicating widespread disapproval.
  • 渐不与游 - Means gradually no one wanted to associate with him.
  • 其兄割资食之 - Means his brother cut off his own resources to support him.
  • 有所如皆枳棘 - Means wherever he went, he encountered thorns and brambles, symbolizing obstacles and difficulties.
  • 益卞急自恣 - Means he became increasingly impulsive and unrestrained.
  • 弃书不读 - Means he abandoned his studies.
  • 但好《世说》《水浒》 - Means he only liked the *Shi Shuo Xin Yu* (A New Account of the Tales of the World) and *Shui Hu Zhuan* (Water Margin), two famous classical novels.
  • 谁敢扣若爷门耶 - Means 'Who dares to knock on your father's door?' indicating a disrespectful and confrontational attitude.
  • 谁为我？我为谁 - Means 'Who am I? For whom?' expressing confusion and self-doubt.
  • 急取大棒击其胫 - Means he quickly took a large stick and hit the person's shin, showing violent behavior.
  • 避我耳 - Means 'Avoid me,' indicating a sense of superiority.
  • 答詈则相搏 - Means if someone answered back, they would fight.
  • 骄我耶 - Means 'Are you showing off to me?' indicating a challenge to authority.
  • 挂阂 - Means to encounter obstacles or difficulties.
  • 以所搏人自嫁于众 - Means he boasted about the people he had fought to the crowd, seeking validation.
  • 彼为彼妻之厚我也，而仇我 - Means 'They treated me well because they wanted to marry me, but now they hate me,' indicating a twisted logic.
  • 岂予罪哉 - Means 'Is it my fault?' expressing self-justification.
  • 袖中一物 - Means an item in his sleeve, likely a piece of evidence or a token.
  • 臂饰 - Refers to an arm ornament, possibly a bracelet or cuff.
  • 誂我者也 - Means 'It was meant to entice me,' suggesting a hidden meaning or intent.
  • 轻薄者竞传之 - Means the frivolous people spread it around, indicating gossip and rumors.
  • 剧言苦语 - Means harsh and bitter words, indicating strong negative reactions.
  • 各以加人 - Means each person used it to criticize others, indicating a chain reaction of criticism.
  • 遂令邑少洁门 - Means it ultimately led to a decline in the moral standards of the town, indicating a broader social impact.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 《世说》 - Refers to *Shi Shuo Xin Yu* (A New Account of the Tales of the World), a collection of anecdotes about the lives of scholars and officials during the Six Dynasties period.
  • 《水浒》 - Refers to *Shui Hu Zhuan* (Water Margin), a classic novel about a group of outlaws and their adventures.

  • 洁门 - Refers to the moral standards or integrity of the town, indicating a social value.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 其妻，中庸人也 - His wife was an ordinary, unremarkable person. '中庸人' refers to someone who is average or unexceptional.
  • 手格之 - Fought back physically; he physically resisted or defended himself.
  • 吾厚其妻，尔乃厚其夫乎 - I treat my wife well, and you treat your husband well? A rhetorical question emphasizing the wife's role in the household.
  • 心诽之 - Secretly criticized him; indicates internal disapproval without expressing it openly.
  • 已而邑之人皆知其诡也 - Later, the people of the town all knew of his deceit; '诡' means deceitful or cunning behavior.
  • 家相告曰 - Families warned each other; meaning they spread the word among themselves.
  • 慎毋与四氏子游 - Be careful not to associate with the Four Surnamed Man; a warning to avoid him.
  • 有与立谈者，死期必至矣 - If anyone stands and talks with him, their death is certain; a hyperbolic warning about the dangers of associating with him.
  • 此秽豕也 - This is a filthy pig; a derogatory comparison.
  • 昔有犬豕卧偃厕中，见狮子过，则负溲溺以侮之，狮子不敢近也 - Once there was a dog and a pig lying in a latrine, when they saw a lion passing by, they carried urine and feces to mock it, and the lion dared not approach. A fable-like anecdote illustrating the idea of a lowly creature mocking a powerful one.
  • 今氏子负秽来，谨避之而已，勿与角也 - Now the Four Surnamed Man comes carrying filth, so be cautious and avoid him, do not engage with him; a warning to avoid confrontation.
  • 居都会中，若空庐 - He lived in the city as if it were an empty house; meaning he was isolated and avoided by others.
  • 行巷市间，唯逢鸡犬草木，不能逢一人也 - When he walked through the streets, he only met chickens, dogs, and plants, but no people; indicating his complete isolation.
  • 游益困 - He became increasingly isolated; meaning his social standing deteriorated.
  • 念《世说》中祖珽获髻上叵罗、袖中金叠 - He thought of the *Shi Shuo Xin Yu* (New Accounts of the Tales of the World) where Zu Ting obtained a comb on his head and gold in his sleeves; a reference to a story in the *Shi Shuo Xin Yu*.
  • 因遇物即怀之 - So whenever he met something, he would take it; indicating his tendency to steal.
  • 人或率众追夺 - Sometimes people would chase after him in groups to take back what he had stolen; describing the reactions of others to his theft.
  • 指名于千百人之前 - He would be named in front of hundreds of people; meaning he would be publicly identified as the thief.
  • 他人丑之 - Others would be ashamed of him; indicating the social stigma attached to his actions.
  • 思入壁罅 - He would think of hiding in a crack in the wall; a metaphor for wanting to disappear or escape.
  • 坦然徐步，不以屑意也 - He would walk calmly and slowly, not caring at all; indicating his lack of concern for the consequences of his actions.
  • 欲作南塘夜出梁山筑栅之事 - He wanted to do something like the story of the Southern Pond at night, building a fence on Liang Mountain; a reference to a specific historical or literary event.
  • 终岁召人，人无肯与同役者 - He called people all year, but no one was willing to work with him; indicating his complete isolation and lack of social support.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 《世说》 - Refers to the *Shi Shuo Xin Yu* (New Accounts of the Tales of the World), a collection of anecdotes about the lives and conversations of notable figures from the late Han to the Eastern Jin dynasties.
  • 祖珽 - A historical figure mentioned in the *Shi Shuo Xin Yu*, known for his cleverness and trickery.
  • 南塘夜出梁山筑栅 - A specific historical or literary event, likely referring to a story of someone building a fence at night in a remote location, possibly as a metaphor for isolation or eccentric behavior.

  • 祖珽 - A historical figure mentioned in the *Shi Shuo Xin Yu*.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 如此十余年 - Time phrase meaning 'for more than ten years,' indicating a long duration.
  • 颇自悔 - Means 'he began to regret' or 'he started to feel remorse.'
  • 其所亲因从容语之曰 - A polite and indirect way of saying 'his close associates gently told him,' where '从容' conveys a calm and respectful tone.
  • 若为儒，而挝父母，何也？ - A rhetorical question asking 'Why did you, as a scholar, strike your parents?'
  • 吾与父母戏耳，何尝尽力挞之哉？ - A defensive response meaning 'I was just playing with my parents; I never hit them hard!'
  • 且悔挝之，必沽酒以释之 - Means 'Moreover, if I regret hitting them, I will buy wine to make amends.'
  • 若詈兄嫂，何也？ - Another rhetorical question asking 'Why did you curse your older brothers and sisters-in-law?'
  • 吾亦戏耳！且子视吾兄嫂之身，有吾詈迹者，吾当罪 - A defensive response meaning 'I was just joking! Moreover, if you look at my older brothers and sisters-in-law, you won’t find any traces of my cursing; I would be guilty if there were.'
  • 子之尽绝六亲百朋，又何也？ - A rhetorical question asking 'Why did you completely cut off ties with your relatives and friends?'
  • 吾初皆戏耳。乃吾六亲百朋，无一达人，见我辄物而不化。彼绝我，我宁绝彼耶？ - A defensive response meaning 'I was just joking at first. But my relatives and friends are all ordinary people; they see me and treat me as an object, not transforming me. If they cut me off, why shouldn’t I cut them off?'
  • 子每诋通人达士，以为不如子，又奈何？ - A rhetorical question asking 'You always criticize accomplished scholars and officials, thinking they are inferior to you. What should I do about that?'
  • 尽戏也。吾戏言江水不如吾沼，江与沼不移位，岂非戏耶？ - A defensive response meaning 'It’s all just joking. I jokingly said that the river is not as good as my pond. The river and the pond don’t change their positions, so isn’t that just a joke?'
  • 顾怨尤侘傺乃尔耶？ - Means 'Why are you so resentful and dejected about it?'
  • 氏子默然无以应 - Means 'The man remained silent and had no response.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 儒 - Refers to Confucian scholars, emphasizing the character's self-identification as a learned person.
  • 挝父母 - A shocking act of physically striking one's parents, which is highly taboo in Confucian culture.
  • 詈兄嫂 - Refers to cursing one's older brothers and sisters-in-law, another act that goes against filial piety and respect for elders.
  • 六亲百朋 - Refers to extended family and friends, a broad social network in traditional Chinese culture.
  • 通人达士 - Refers to accomplished scholars and officials, individuals who are respected for their wisdom and virtue.
  • 江水不如吾沼 - A metaphorical statement comparing the river (symbolizing the outside world or broader society) to a pond (symbolizing the character’s own domain or perspective), suggesting a subjective and biased view.

Proper Nouns

  • 氏子 - Refers to the character being described, likely a commoner or scholar, but no specific name is given.
  • 通人达士 - Refers to accomplished scholars and officials, but no specific individuals are mentioned.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 无何 - Classical adverb meaning 'soon after' or 'shortly', indicating a brief passage of time.
  • 少亦韶令 - '少' means 'young', and '韶令' means 'handsome' or 'graceful', describing the son's appearance.
  • 将弱忽得狂疾 - '将弱' means 'when he was young', and '忽得狂疾' means 'suddenly fell ill with a mad disease', indicating a sudden onset of mental illness.
  • 终日喃喃詈人 - '终日' means 'all day', and '喃喃詈人' means 'muttering curses at people', describing the son's behavior.
  • 则皆其父也 - '则皆' means 'all', and '其父' means 'his father', indicating that the son was cursing his father.
  • 其父至，则枚数其罪而挞之 - '枚数' means 'enumerate' or 'list', and '挞之' means 'beat him', describing the father's response.
  • 或言惨于氏子父被挞时 - '或言' means 'some say', and '惨于氏子父被挞时' means 'more cruel than when the father was beaten', indicating a comparison of cruelty.
  • 械子囚诸室 - '械子' means 'imprison him', and '囚诸室' means 'lock him in a room', describing the father's punishment.
  • 则以一木为其父 - '则以一木' means 'then use a wooden figure', and '为其父' means 'representing his father', indicating the use of a wooden figure.
  • 诘之曰 - '诘之' means 'question him', and '曰' means 'say', indicating the father's questioning.
  • 父母可挝乎 - '父母' means 'parents', and '可挝乎' means 'can they be beaten?', indicating the question posed to the son.
  • 代应之曰 - '代应之' means 'answer on his behalf', and '曰' means 'say', indicating the son's response.
  • 是宜挞 - '是' means 'this', and '宜挞' means 'should be beaten', indicating the son's response.
  • 日挞至百数 - '日挞' means 'daily beating', and '至百数' means 'up to a hundred times', describing the frequency and severity of the beating.
  • 其余罪皆然 - '其余罪' means 'other offenses', and '皆然' means 'all were treated the same', indicating that all offenses were punished similarly.
  • 数年，竟狂死 - '数年' means 'after several years', and '竟狂死' means 'eventually died from madness', indicating the son's tragic end.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 氏子 - Refers to the son of a certain family, emphasizing the familial context.
  • 狂疾 - Refers to a mental illness, which was often attributed to supernatural causes in ancient times.
  • 械子囚诸室 - Describes a form of punishment where the son was imprisoned in a room, reflecting the severity of the father's actions.
  • 木为其父 - Refers to a wooden figure representing the father, which was used to teach the son a lesson.
  • 父母可挝乎 - Questions the appropriateness of beating one's parents, reflecting the moral and ethical dilemmas of the time.
  • 竟狂死 - Indicates the son's eventual death from madness, which was a tragic outcome in ancient Chinese society.

Proper Nouns

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • Introduces a commentary or reflection, which should be clearly marked in the translation.
  • This long descriptive passage should be broken down into manageable segments to maintain clarity.
  • This series of comparisons should be translated in a way that preserves the parallel structure.
  • This clause explains why the person is called '四氏子,' and should be clearly linked to the previous descriptions.
  • This series of rhetorical questions should be translated to maintain the questioning tone.
  • This complex sentence should be broken down to clarify the relationships and actions described.
  • This sentence describes a father's actions as being cruel and precise, and should be translated to convey this nuance.
  • This concluding statement should be translated to maintain the rhetorical tone.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 东海生曰 - Classical style of authorial voice, meaning 'The Eastern Sea Scholar says' or 'The scholar from the East Sea says.'
  • 可谓不负虞山矣哉 - Rhetorical exclamation meaning 'can truly be said to have not failed or disappointed Yu Mountain.'
  • 情之所钟，生怜死捐，缠绵毕命 - Classical expression describing intense emotional attachment that leads to a life and death commitment.
  • 连理枝 - Twined branches, symbolizing a couple joined in love.
  • 雉朝飞 - The pheasant flying at dawn, a reference to a legend of a wife who waited for her husband and eventually died, symbolizing loyalty and devotion.
  • 双鸳鸯 - Two mandarin ducks, a traditional symbol of marital harmony and fidelity.
  • 七尺腐躯，归于等尽 - Metaphor for the human body as a temporary, decaying form.
  • 掷之当 - Phrase implying 'throwing oneself into the right cause' or 'acting at the right moment.'
  • 侯赢以存弱赵，杵臼以立藐孤，秀实以缓奉天之危，纪信以脱荥阳之难 - Historical allusions to figures who made heroic sacrifices for their states or people.
  • 或轻于鸿羽，或重于泰山 - Classical metaphor from the *Zhuangzi* and other texts: 'Light as a feather, heavy as a mountain.'
  • 柳夫人以尺组下报尚书，而纾其身后之祸 - Metaphorical statement about the actions of Liu’s wife.
  • 所云重用其死者也 - 'This is what is meant by the value of using the dead in service.'
  • 西陵松柏，才矣，未闻择所从 - Reference to the tomb of Cao Cao (Xiling) and its cypress trees, symbolizing enduring legacy.
  • 耆卿、月仙，齐丘、散花女 - Names of historical or legendary figures associated with love and tragedy.
  • 韩香、幼玉、张红红、罗爱爱 - Names of historical or legendary women known for their beauty or tragic fates.
  • 千秋香躅 - 'The fragrant footsteps of a thousand autumns,' a metaphor for enduring reputation or legacy.
  • 唯张尚书燕子一楼 - 'Only Zhang the Minister's Swallow Tower,' a reference to Zhang Sheng, a historical or literary figure associated with a tower and a tragic love story.
  • 红粉成灰，尚在白杨可柱之后 - 'The red powder (a metaphor for beauty or women) becomes ashes, still after the white poplar can support a column.'
  • 玉容黄土之不惜，而顾以从死之名为地下虑，荒矣 - 'Not sparing the jade-like face to turn to dust, yet concerning oneself with the name of following in death for the underworld—this is indeed misguided.'
  • 微曰舍人，泉台下随，未敢必其然也 - 'If one were to say that the *sheren* (a title for a court official) would follow in the afterlife, I dare not be certain of that.'
  • 人固不可知，千寻之操，或以一念隳；生平之疵，或以晚节覆 - 'People are indeed unknowable. A virtue that was once lofty may be destroyed by a single thought; a lifetime of flaws may be covered by a final virtue.'
  • 遂志赴义，争乎一决 - 'To follow one's will to righteousness, to decide in one moment.'
  • 柳夫人存不必称，而没以馨，委蜕如遗，岂不壮哉 - 'Liu’s wife need not be praised for her life, but her death is fragrant, her body laid to rest like a forgotten relic—how magnificent is this!'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 东海生 - A pseudonym or self-identification used by the author, likely a scholar or commentator writing in a classical style.
  • 虞山 - A mountain in Jiangsu province, traditionally associated with cultural and historical significance.
  • 侯赢 - A historical figure from the Warring States period, a retainer of the state of Zhao who famously committed suicide to honor his lord’s honor.
  • 杵臼 - A metaphor for the loyal and self-sacrificing actions of a woman.
  • 秀实 - Possibly a reference to Zhang Xiu Shi (张秀实), a figure from the Tang Dynasty, or a metaphor for someone who saved the state in a time of crisis.
  • 纪信 - A historical figure from the Han Dynasty who impersonated Liu Bang to allow him to escape from the siege of Yingyang, sacrificing his life for the state.
  • 西陵 - The tomb of Cao Cao, located near Luoyang. The cypress trees there are often symbolic of enduring legacy.
  • 齐丘 - A name that may refer to the legendary figure from the *Zhou Li* (Rites of Zhou) or a historical person known for his virtue or tragedy.
  • 散花女 - A reference to a Buddhist or romantic figure, possibly from the *Journey to the West* or other Buddhist legends.
  • 韩香 - Possibly a reference to Han Xiang, a historical figure from the *Book of Han* or a romantic figure from poetry.
  • 张尚书 - A reference to Zhang Sheng, a historical or literary figure associated with a tower and a tragic love story.
  • 红粉成灰 - A metaphor for the fading of beauty or virtue after death.
  • 白杨 - White poplar trees, often used in Chinese literature to symbolize mourning.
  • 千秋香躅 - A reference to the enduring legacy of virtue or beauty.
  • 泉台 - A term for the afterlife or the underworld.
  • 千寻之操 - 'A virtue of a thousand feet,' a metaphor for high moral character.
  • 晚节 - 'Late virtue' or 'final moral standing.'
  • 委蜕如遗 - 'To lay down the body like a forgotten relic.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 柳夫人可以不死矣 - Hyperbolic expression indicating that the person has achieved a significant or admirable state, to the point where they can be considered 'immortal' or beyond ordinary human fate.
  • 柳萦花笑 - Metaphorical expression evoking a scene of beauty and grace, often used to describe something as delightful or charming.
  • 笳响剑鸣 - Metaphorical expression conveying a sense of martial valor and heroic spirit, often used to describe a scene of battle or a person with a warrior's spirit.
  • 负文名 - Literally means 'to bear the reputation of being a literary figure,' indicating someone well-known for their literary achievements.
  • 教之读书 - Means 'to teach him to read,' but in context, implies a broader education.
  • 性奇僿 - Means 'has an odd and simple nature,' suggesting that the person is somewhat naive or unsophisticated.
  • 咿唔十数载 - Describes the sound of reading aloud for many years, often used to indicate long periods of study.
  • 寻常书卷 - Refers to ordinary books or texts, suggesting that the person cannot even handle basic reading material.
  • 懵如故 - Means 'as confused as before,' indicating that the person remains unintelligent or unenlightened despite efforts to educate them.
  • 岁暮辞去 - Means 'to leave at the end of the year,' indicating that the teacher is departing after a long period of teaching.
  • 某力竭矣 - Means 'I have exhausted my strength,' indicating that the teacher feels they have done all they can.
  • 叔产固丰 - Means 'my uncle's wealth is indeed abundant,' indicating that the uncle is wealthy.
  • 不失田舍翁 - Means 'will not be a mere farmer or landowner,' suggesting that the son will at least have a comfortable life.
  • 趣治具饯师 - Means 'to prepare a feast to send off the teacher,' indicating a formal farewell.
  • 私觅大梃 - Means 'to secretly find a large stick,' suggesting that the father is preparing to use violence.
  • 若有所待 - Means 'as if waiting for something,' indicating that the father is prepared for a confrontation.
  • 盖公恨进士辱己 - Means 'it seems that the old man hates the scholar for disgracing him,' indicating the father's deep resentment.
  • 意且扑杀之 - Means 'intends to beat him to death,' indicating the father's extreme anger.
  • 以产施僧寺 - Means 'to give his property to temples,' indicating that the father is considering giving up his wealth.
  • 作终老计 - Means 'to plan for a long life,' suggesting that the father is considering a life of seclusion or retirement.
  • 呼进士窃语 - Means 'to call the scholar and speak to him privately,' indicating a secret conversation.
  • 使他避 - Means 'to tell him to avoid,' suggesting that the mother is advising the son to stay away.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 万历中 - Refers to the middle period of the Wanli era, which was during the Ming Dynasty.
  • 徽州进士 - Refers to a scholar from Huizhou who passed the imperial examination, indicating a person of high social status.
  • 性卞急 - Describes a person who is quick-tempered and impatient, a common trait attributed to some historical figures.
  • 家故饶赀 - Means 'the family was originally wealthy,' indicating that the family had a history of wealth.
  • 不谐于族 - Means 'does not get along with the clan,' suggesting that the family has conflicts with other members of the clan.
  • 足两腓瘦削无肉 - Describes the physical appearance of the father, indicating that his calves are thin and bony.
  • 生不肖子 - Means 'to have an unworthy son,' a common expression of disappointment in a parent.
  • 趣治具饯师 - Refers to a formal farewell, a common practice in traditional Chinese culture.
  • 私觅大梃 - Indicates the father's preparation for a violent act, reflecting the severity of his anger.
  • 以产施僧寺 - Suggests a common practice of giving wealth to temples as a form of religious devotion or to escape personal problems.
  • 作终老计 - Indicates a common practice of planning for a long life, often in the context of retirement or seclusion.
  • 呼进士窃语 - Reflects the secretive nature of family matters, especially when dealing with sensitive issues.
  • 使他避 - Suggests a common practice of advising someone to stay away from a dangerous situation.

Proper Nouns

  • 万历 - The era name of the Wanli Emperor, who ruled the Ming Dynasty from 1572 to 1620.
  • 徽州 - A region in modern-day Anhui Province, known for its scholars and merchants.
  • 进士 - A person who has passed the highest level of the imperial examination, indicating a high social status.
  • 张山来 - The author or compiler of the text, whose name is mentioned at the beginning.
  • 徐芳仲光诺皋广志 - The title of the work, which is a collection of stories or anecdotes.
  • 万历中 - The time period during the Wanli era.
  • 徽州进士某太翁 - The main character, an old man from Huizhou who is a former scholar.
  • 进士公 - The son of the main character, who is also a scholar.
  • 远族侄某 - A distant relative of the main character, who is a teacher.
  • 柳夫人 - A metaphorical reference to a woman of high status, often used in classical Chinese literature.
  • 叔产固丰 - Refers to the uncle's wealth.
  • 田舍翁 - A term for a farmer or landowner, often used to describe a simple, rural life.
  • 趣治具饯师 - A formal farewell, a common practice in traditional Chinese culture.
  • 私觅大梃 - A large stick, used in the context of the father's preparation for a violent act.
  • 若有所待 - As if waiting for something, indicating the father's preparedness.
  • 盖公恨进士辱己 - The old man's deep resentment towards his son.
  • 意且扑杀之 - Intending to beat him to death, indicating the father's extreme anger.
  • 以产施僧寺 - Giving his property to temples, a common practice of religious devotion.
  • 作终老计 - Planning for a long life, often in the context of retirement or seclusion.
  • 呼进士窃语 - Calling the scholar and speaking to him privately, reflecting the secretive nature of family matters.
  • 使他避 - Telling him to avoid, suggesting a common practice of advising someone to stay away from a dangerous situation.

[158]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 进士甫新娶 - The newly married scholar-official, where '甫' (fǔ) indicates 'just' or 'recently.'
  • 是夜合户筹议 - That night, after closing the door, they discussed, where '合户' (hé hù) means 'close the door' and '筹议' (chóu yì) means 'discuss' or 'deliberate.'
  • 欲留；恐祸不测，欲去，无所之 - Wanted to stay; but feared unforeseen disaster, wanted to leave, but had nowhere to go, where '欲' (yù) means 'want to,' '恐' (kǒng) means 'fear,' '祸不测' (huò bù cè) means 'unforeseen disaster,' and '无所之' (wú suǒ zhī) means 'nowhere to go.'
  • 则夫妇相持大哭 - So the couple held each other and cried, where '相持' (xiāng chí) means 'hold each other' and '大哭' (dà kū) means 'cry loudly.'
  • 不觉夜半 - Without realizing it was midnight, where '不觉' (bù jué) means 'without realizing' and '夜半' (yè bàn) means 'midnight.'
  • 倦极假寐 - Tired to the point of dozing off, where '倦极' (juàn jí) means 'tired to the extreme' and '假寐' (jiǎ mèi) means 'doze off.'
  • 见有金甲神拥巨斧，排闼入 - Saw a god in golden armor wielding a great axe, breaking in through the door, where '金甲神' (jīn jiǎ shén) means 'god in golden armor,' '拥巨斧' (yōng jù fǔ) means 'wielding a great axe,' and '排闼入' (pái tà rù) means 'break in through the door.'
  • 捽其胸，劈之，抉其心出 - Grabbed his chest, split it open, and pulled out his heart, where '捽其胸' (zhuó qí xiōng) means 'grabbed his chest,' '劈之' (pī zhī) means 'split it open,' and '抉其心出' (jué qí xīn chū) means 'pulled out his heart.'
  • 又别取一心纳之 - Then took another heart and put it in, where '别取' (bié qǔ) means 'took another' and '纳之' (nà zhī) means 'put it in.'
  • 大惊而寤 - Greatly startled and woke up, where '大惊' (dà jīng) means 'greatly startled' and '寤' (wù) means 'woke up.'
  • 次日，翁延侄饮为别 - The next day, the old man invited his nephew to drink as a farewell, where '次日' (cì rì) means 'the next day,' '翁' (wēng) means 'old man,' '延' (yán) means 'invite,' '侄' (zhí) means 'nephew,' and '饮为别' (yǐn wéi bié) means 'drink as a farewell.'
  • 师矍然曰 - The teacher looked startled and said, where '矍然' (jué rán) means 'looked startled.'
  • 安得此达者言 - How could you say something so wise? where '安得' (ān dé) means 'how could' and '达者言' (dá zhě yán) means 'wise person's words.'
  • 此自某意 - This is my own idea, where '自' (zì) means 'own' and '意' (yì) means 'idea.'
  • 颇觉胸次开朗 - Feeling quite open-minded, where '颇觉' (pō jué) means 'feeling quite' and '胸次开朗' (xiōng cì kāi lǎng) means 'open-minded.'
  • 愿更从师卒业 - Wish to continue studying with the teacher until completion, where '愿' (yuàn) means 'wish,' '更从师' (gèng cóng shī) means 'continue studying with the teacher,' and '卒业' (zú yè) means 'until completion.'
  • 因述夜来梦 - Because he described the dream he had last night, where '因' (yīn) means 'because,' '述' (shù) means 'describe,' and '夜来梦' (yè lái mèng) means 'dream he had last night.'
  • 师叩以所授书，辄能记诵 - The teacher asked him about the books he had been taught, and he could immediately recall and recite them, where '叩以所授书' (kòu yǐ suǒ shòu shū) means 'asked him about the books he had been taught,' and '辄能记诵' (zhé néng jì sòng) means 'could immediately recall and recite them.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 进士 - A scholar-official who has passed the imperial examination, indicating a high level of education and social status.
  • 翁 - An elderly man, often used to refer to a father or grandfather.
  • 师 - A teacher or mentor, often a respected figure in traditional Chinese society.
  • 金甲神 - A god in golden armor, often associated with deities in Chinese mythology.
  • 排闼入 - Breaking in through the door, a dramatic and forceful action.
  • 抉其心出 - Pulling out the heart, a symbolic act of removing the soul or life force.
  • 夜来梦 - A dream experienced during the night, often used to convey supernatural or symbolic experiences.

Proper Nouns

  • 进士 - Scholar-official.
  • 翁 - Old man.
  • 师 - Teacher.
  • 金甲神 - God in golden armor.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 翁聞剥啄聲，掣梃門俟 - 翁 refers to an elderly man; 聞 means to hear; 剥啄聲 describes the sound of someone knocking at the door; 掣梃 means to grab a stick or weapon; 門俟 means to wait by the door.
  • 已聞師返，則延入 - 已 means already; 聞 means to hear; 師返 means the teacher has returned; 則 means then; 延入 means to invite in.
  • 師具以途中所聞告 - 具 means fully or in detail; 途中所聞 means what was heard on the way.
  • 翁以為謬，試之良然，乃大喜 - 以為 means to think; 謬 means wrong or mistaken; 試之 means to test it; 良然 means indeed so; 乃 means then; 大喜 means greatly pleased.
  • 自是敏穎大著，不數歲，補邑諸生 - 自是 means from that point on; 敏穎 means intelligent and quick-witted; 大著 means greatly evident; 不數歲 means within a few years; 補邑諸生 means to be enrolled as a student in the local school.
  • 又數歲，聯捷成進士 - 又數歲 means another few years; 聯捷 means to succeed continuously; 成進士 means to become a jinshi (imperial scholar-official).
  • 報至之日，翁坐胡床，大笑曰 - 報至之日 means the day the news arrived; 胡床 means a type of chair; 大笑曰 means to laugh loudly and say.
  • 乃公自是免於乞矣 - 乃公 means your father; 免於乞 means to be freed from begging.
  • 因張口啞啞而逝 - 因 means thus; 張口啞啞 means to open the mouth and make a sound; 逝 means to die.
  • 族子某為郡從事 - 族子 means a relative; 郡從事 means an official in the prefecture.
  • 庚辰與予遇山左道中 - 庚辰 is a year in the Chinese zodiac; 與予遇 means to meet me; 山左 means the eastern part of the mountain (Shandong); 道中 means on the road.
  • 精誠所激，人窮而神應之 - 精誠 means sincerity and earnestness; 所激 means to be inspired; 人窮 means in dire straits; 神應之 means the gods respond.
  • 進士之奇穎，進士之奇愚逼而出也 - 奇穎 means extraordinary intelligence; 奇愚 means extraordinary foolishness; 逼而出 means to be forced out.
  • 所謂德慧存乎疢疾者也 - 所謂 means so-called; 德慧 means virtue and wisdom; 存乎 means exist in; 疢疾 means illness.
  • 今天下之心，可換者多矣 - 今天下 means today's world; 可換者 means those that can be changed; 多矣 means many.
  • 安得一一捽其胸剖之 - 安得 means how can; 一一 means one by one; 捽其胸 means to grab their chest; 剖之 means to cut open.
  • 易其殘者而使仁 - 易其殘者 means to change the cruel ones; 使仁 means to make them kind.
  • 易其污者而使廉 - 易其污者 means to change the corrupt ones; 使廉 means to make them clean.
  • 易其奸回邪佞者而使忠厚正直 - 易其奸回邪佞者 means to change the deceitful and wicked ones; 使忠厚正直 means to make them loyal, honest, and upright.
  • 若是，神之斧日不暇給矣 - 若是 means if so; 神之斧 means the god's axe; 日不暇給 means not having enough time.
  • 且今天下之心皆是矣 - 且 means moreover; 今天下之心皆是矣 means today's hearts are all like that.
  • 又安所得仁者廉者忠若直者而納之，而因易之哉 - 又安所得 means how can we get; 仁者廉者忠若直者 means kind, clean, loyal, and upright people; 納之 means to accept them; 因易之哉 means to change them.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 翁 - An elderly man, likely a commoner.
  • 師 - A teacher or scholar.
  • 進士 - A high-ranking imperial scholar-official.
  • 胡床 - A type of chair, often associated with comfort and leisure.
  • 庚辰 - A year in the Chinese zodiac, corresponding to 1660 in the Gregorian calendar.
  • 山左 - The eastern part of the mountain, referring to Shandong province.
  • 精誠所激，人窮而神應之 - A belief in the power of sincere and earnest effort to elicit divine intervention.
  • 進士之奇穎，進士之奇愚逼而出也 - A paradoxical statement about the extremes of intelligence and foolishness in a jinshi.
  • 所謂德慧存乎疢疾者也 - A reference to the idea that virtue and wisdom can arise from adversity or illness.
  • 今天下之心，可換者多矣 - A commentary on the potential for moral transformation in society.
  • 神之斧 - A metaphor for divine intervention or judgment.

Proper Nouns

  • 翁 - The elderly man.
  • 師 - The teacher or scholar.
  • 進士 - The high-ranking imperial scholar-official.
  • 胡床 - The type of chair.
  • 庚辰 - The year 1660.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 健兒性善動 - Jiàn'ér (健兒) refers to a young man or warrior, and '性善動' means 'naturally inclined to move or be active.'
  • 不喜读书 - Literally 'does not like to read,' indicating a dislike for scholarly pursuits.
  • 外氏命就外傅 - '外氏' refers to the maternal family, and '命就外傅' means 'ordered to go to a private tutor.'
  • 不率教 - Means 'does not follow the teachings' or 'is disobedient.'
  • 师夏楚之 - '师' (teacher) and '夏楚之' (punishment with a cane or rod) refer to the teacher using corporal punishment.
  • 夺扑裂眦 - '夺扑' means 'to snatch the rod,' and '裂眦' means 'eyes tearing up in pain,' describing a violent reaction to punishment.
  • 功名应赤手致 - '功名' means 'honor and fame,' and '应赤手致' means 'should be achieved without help or tools,' emphasizing self-reliance.
  • 焉用琐琐章句为 - '焉用' means 'why use,' and '琐琐章句' refers to 'trivial phrases and sentences,' suggesting a disdain for rote learning.
  • 即与同塾诸儿斗 - '即' means 'immediately,' '同塾' means 'in the same school,' and '诸儿' means 'other children,' describing immediate conflict with peers.
  • 无完肤 - Means 'no part of the body is left unbruised,' indicating severe physical harm.
  • 又时盗其外氏簪珥衣物 - '盗' means 'steal,' '簪珥' refers to 'hairpins and earrings,' and '衣物' means 'clothes,' describing theft from the maternal family.
  • 向酒家饮 - '向' means 'to,' and '酒家' means 'tavern,' indicating drinking at an inn.
  • 醉即猖狂生事 - '醉' means 'drunk,' '猖狂' means 'reckless,' and '生事' means 'create trouble,' describing drunken behavior.
  • 外氏苦之，逐于外 - '苦之' means 'suffered from him,' and '逐于外' means 'expelled him,' indicating the maternal family's frustration and expulsion.
  • 为人牧羊 - '为人' means 'to serve as,' and '牧羊' means 'to herd sheep,' describing a job as a shepherd.
  • 每窃羊换饮 - '每' means 'often,' '窃羊' means 'steal sheep,' and '换饮' means 'exchange for drinks,' indicating frequent theft.
  • 诈言多歧亡 - '诈言' means 'false claim,' '多歧' means 'many paths,' and '亡' means 'lost,' suggesting a fabricated excuse for lost sheep.
  • 主人怒，复见摈 - '主人' means 'master,' '怒' means 'angry,' and '复见摈' means 'again expelled,' indicating further expulsion.
  • 时已弱冠矣 - '弱冠' means 'reached adulthood' (around 20 years old).
  • 闻倭入寇 - '倭' refers to the Japanese, and '入寇' means 'invade,' indicating news of a Japanese invasion.
  • 大快曰 - '大快' means 'greatly pleased,' and '曰' means 'said,' expressing satisfaction.
  • 即去海上从军 - '即' means 'immediately,' '去海上' means 'to the sea,' and '从军' means 'join the army,' describing immediate enlistment.
  • 从小校擢功至裨将 - '从小校' means 'from a low-ranking officer,' '擢功' means 'promoted through merit,' and '至裨将' means 'to the rank of deputy general,' indicating rapid promotion.
  • 与僚友饮，酒酣斗力，毙之 - '与僚友' means 'with fellow officers,' '酒酣' means 'drunk,' '斗力' means 'compete in strength,' and '毙之' means 'kill him,' describing a violent incident.
  • 罪当死，遂弃官，逃之泗，易姓名，隐于庖丁 - '罪当死' means 'guilty of a capital crime,' '遂弃官' means 'abandoned his post,' '逃之泗' means 'fled to Si' (a place), '易姓名' means 'changed his name,' and '隐于庖丁' means 'hid as a butcher,' indicating escape and hiding.
  • 民家有犊，丙夜往盗之，牵出，必剧呼曰 - '民家' means 'a commoner's house,' '有犊' means 'has a calf,' '丙夜' means 'midnight,' '往盗之' means 'went to steal it,' '牵出' means 'dragged it out,' and '必剧呼曰' means 'certainly shouted loudly,' describing a nighttime theft.
  • 君家牛我骑去矣 - '君家牛' means 'your cow,' '我骑去矣' means 'I rode it away,' indicating a boastful claim.
  • 呼竟，倒骑牛背，以斧砍牛臀 - '呼竟' means 'shouted until,' '倒骑牛背' means 'rode backward on the cow's back,' and '以斧砍牛臀' means 'hit the cow's rump with an axe,' describing a violent act.
  • 牛畏痛，迅奔若风，追之莫及 - '牛畏痛' means 'the cow was afraid of pain,' '迅奔若风' means 'ran quickly like the wind,' and '追之莫及' means 'could not catch it,' indicating the cow's escape.
  • 次日亡牛者适市物色之 - '次日' means 'the next day,' '亡牛者' means 'the owner of the lost cow,' '适市' means 'went to the market,' and '物色之' means 'looked for it,' describing the search.
  • 健儿曰 - '健儿' means 'the young man,' and '曰' means 'said,' indicating his response.
  • 昨过君家取牛者我也，告而后取，道也，奚其盗 - '昨过君家取牛者我也' means 'I was the one who took your cow yesterday,' '告而后取' means 'informed you before taking it,' '道也' means 'it is the right way,' and '奚其盗' means 'why call it stealing,' justifying his actions.
  • 索之，则牛已脯矣，无可凭 - '索之' means 'searched for it,' '则牛已脯矣' means 'the cow had already been made into jerky,' and '无可凭' means 'there was no evidence,' indicating the impossibility of proving theft.
  • 市中恶少，推为盟主 - '市中恶少' means 'bad boys in the market,' and '推为盟主' means 'elected as the leader,' describing leadership among troublemakers.
  • 昼纵六博，夜游狭斜 - '昼纵六博' means 'played six-sided dice during the day,' and '夜游狭斜' means 'wandered the alleys at night,' indicating reckless behavior.
  • 自恃日甚 - '自恃' means 'rely on oneself,' and '日甚' means 'increasingly,' describing growing arrogance.
  • 尝叹曰 - '尝' means 'once,' '叹曰' means 'said with regret,' indicating a moment of reflection.
  • 世人皆不足敌，但恨生千载后，不得与拔山举鼎之雄一较胜负耳 - '世人皆不足敌' means 'ordinary people are not worth fighting,' '但恨生千载后' means 'but regret being born so many years later,' '不得与拔山举鼎之雄一较胜负耳' means 'cannot compete with the heroes who could move mountains and lift cauldrons,' expressing a desire to compete with legendary heroes.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 外氏 - Refers to the maternal family, indicating a relationship outside the paternal lineage.

  • 师夏楚之 - Refers to the use of corporal punishment in education, a common practice in traditional Chinese schools.
  • 功名 - Refers to honor and fame, often associated with military and scholarly achievements.

  • 六博 - Refers to a type of dice game, popular in ancient China.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 邑使者禁屠牛 - Literal phrase meaning 'the town official banned the slaughter of cattle.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 泗上诸英雄 - Refers to heroes in the Si River region.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '邑使者禁屠牛，健儿无所事事' introduces the background of the story and the situation of the strong man.
  • The clause '取向所屠牛皮及骨角，往瓜扬间售之，得三十金' describes the actions of the strong man and the outcome of his efforts.
  • The clause '将归，饮于馆中，解金置案头' describes the strong man's return and his actions in the inn.
  • The clause '酒家翁见之，谓曰：“前途多豪客，此物宜善藏之。”' introduces the innkeeper's warning.
  • The clause '健儿掷杯砍案曰：“吾纵横天下三十年，未逢敌手，有能取得腰间物者，当叩首降之。”' describes the strong man's reaction to the innkeeper's warning.
  • The clause '时有少年数人，醵于左席，闻之错愕，起问姓名居里' introduces the young men and their reaction to the strong man's challenge.
  • The clause '某姓名不传，向尝竖功于边陲，今挂冠微服，牛耳于泗上诸英雄' provides the strong man's background and current status.
  • The clause '少年问能敌几何辈，健儿曰：遇万万敌，遇千千敌。计人而敌，斯下矣！' describes the young men's question and the strong man's response.
  • The clause '诸少年益错愕' concludes the story with the young men's reaction.

Proper Nouns

  • 邑使者 - The local official or magistrate.
  • 健兒 - The strong or brave man.
  • 瓜扬间 - The region or market area.
  • 酒家翁 - The innkeeper.
  • 豪客 - Bold and strong men.
  • 挂冠微服 - Resigned and living in civilian clothes.
  • 牛耳 - Leadership or authority.
  • 泗上诸英雄 - Heroes in the Si River region.

  • 匍伏請所欲 - Crawl and beg for what is desired; describes the act of pleading humbly.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 遂出左袖三十金置案頭 - Classical narrative construction meaning 'He then took out thirty gold pieces from his left sleeve and placed them on the table.'
  • 此母也。于今一年，子當肖之 - Statement of intent and promise. 'This is the mother. In one year, the child will resemble her.' '肖之' is an idiom meaning 'to resemble' or 'to imitate.'
  • 健兒不敢受 - 'The strong man dared not accept' — a common expression of hesitation or fear.
  • 旁一后生拔劍努目曰 - 'A young man nearby drew his sword and glared, saying...' — a typical way to introduce a character's dramatic action and speech in Classical Chinese.
  • 安用此懦夫為 - 'What use is this coward?' — a rhetorical question expressing contempt.
  • 乃治雞黍為歡 - 'Then he prepared chicken and millet to celebrate' — a common expression for a simple, modest feast or celebration.
  • 歸金者曰 - 'The one who returned the gold said' — a way to introduce a character's dialogue.
  • 翁亦可怜矣，峻拒之則難堪 - 'The old man is also pitiable. To reject him harshly would be awkward.' — a polite and tactful expression.
  • 時爨下薪窮 - 'At that time, the firewood in the stove was exhausted' — a way to describe a situation of scarcity.
  • 盍載斧斤 - 'Why not bring an axe?' — a rhetorical question suggesting a solution.
  • 正苦無斧斤耳 - 'Exactly, we are suffering from not having an axe.' — a direct response to the suggestion.
  • 此事須讓十弟，我九人無能為也 - 'This matter must be left to the tenth brother; the rest of us nine cannot do it.' — a polite way of deferring to another person's ability.
  • 總角者以兩手抱株，左右數撓，株已臥矣 - 'The child with braids (a young boy) took the tree in both hands, struck it several times from the sides, and the tree fell.' — a vivid description of manual labor.
  • 遂拔劍砍旁柯燃之 - 'He then drew his sword and cut branches from the side to light the fire.' — a sequence of actions in a narrative.
  • 酒至無算，乃辭去 - 'When the wine was poured without measure, they then departed.' — a poetic way of describing a drinking session and its conclusion.
  • 竟不知其何許人 - 'In the end, it was unknown who they were.' — a common narrative closure in Classical Chinese, suggesting mystery or anonymity.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 健兒 - A term for a strong or capable man, possibly a warrior or a hired soldier.
  • 后生 - A term for a young man or youth, often used in a respectful or dismissive tone depending on context.
  • 三十金 - 'Thirty pieces of gold' — a significant sum in the context of Classical Chinese society, indicating wealth or value.
  • 治雞黍 - 'To prepare chicken and millet' — a humble but traditional meal, often used in narratives to depict modest hospitality or celebration.
  • 爨下薪窮 - 'The firewood in the stove was exhausted' — a reference to a domestic situation, highlighting scarcity and the need to act.
  • 斧斤 - 'Axes' — a tool essential for cutting wood, reflecting the agrarian and manual labor context of the time.
  • 總角者 - A term for a young child, literally meaning 'child with braids,' referring to a child in early childhood.
  • 十弟 - 'Tenth brother' — a familial term, suggesting a group of brothers or a social group with hierarchical roles.
  • 拔劍 - 'Drawing a sword' — a symbolic or literal act of confrontation or defense, common in narratives of honor or conflict.
  • 酒至無算 - 'The wine was poured without measure' — a poetic way of describing an abundance of wine and a convivial atmosphere, often associated with drinking culture in ancient China.

  • 室无空虚 - Literally means 'the room had no emptiness,' implying it was always full of people.
  • 扃鐍甚固 - 扃鐍 refers to locks and bolts, 甚固 means 'very secure,' indicating the garden was tightly locked.
  • 及笄而死 - 及笄 refers to a girl reaching the age of marriage (around 15 years old), 而死 means 'and died,' describing the fate of the deceased daughter.
  • 徘徊徙倚，如不胜情 - 徘徊徙倚 means 'wandering and leaning,' 如不胜情 means 'as if overwhelmed with emotion,' depicting the ghostly figure's movements.
  • 审若是，是故我所祷祀而求者也 - 审若是 means 'if indeed it is so,' 是故我所祷祀而求者也 means 'then this is what I have been praying for and seeking,' expressing the narrator's excitement.
  • 冀有所遇，而卒无见闻 - 冀有所遇 means 'hoping to encounter something,' 而卒无见闻 means 'but ultimately nothing was seen or heard,' describing the outcome.
  • 今林四娘独能变现若此，则又何也？ - 今林四娘独能变现若此 means 'now, only Lin Si娘 can transform like this,' 则又何也 means 'what is the reason for this?' questioning the phenomenon.
  • 岂必无罪而冤死者乃能为厉耶？ - 岂必无罪而冤死者 means 'is it necessarily the case that only those who died unjustly can become vengeful spirits?' 乃能为厉耶 means 'can become vengeful spirits?' raising a moral and supernatural question.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 洒口王氏 - Family name or clan designation, likely referring to a prominent family in the area of Nuo.

  • 而环不知 - "而环不知" means 'but the girl did not know.'

  • 或挞之急，且有变 - "或挞之急" means 'or beats her severely'; "且有变" means 'and there might be trouble.'
  • 吾贫人，横得重资，未必能享，而贻环累，以至不测，大不祥 - "吾贫人" means 'I am a poor person'; "横得重资" means 'unexpectedly gained a valuable item'; "未必能享" means 'may not be able to enjoy it'; "而贻环累" means 'and bring trouble to the girl'; "以至不测" means 'leading to unforeseen consequences'; "大不祥" means 'great misfortune.'
  • 遂留以待 - "遂留以待" means 'so he decided to wait.'
  • 久之，微闻户内喧声 - "久之" means 'after a long time'; "微闻户内喧声" means 'he faintly heard commotion inside the house.'
  • 似有所诃责 - "似有所诃责" means 'as if someone was scolding.'

  • 环入报，主妇以为谩，遣童出问翁，具以实对 - "环入报" means 'the girl went in to report'; "主妇以为谩" means 'the lady thought it was a joke'; "遣童出问翁" means 'sent a child to ask the old man'; "具以实对" means 'the old man told the truth.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 或曰 - Classical introductory phrase meaning 'some say' or 'it is said that,' often used to introduce a question or statement for consideration.
  • 大姓 - Refers to a prominent or influential family, often associated with social status.
  • 祖貧至於乞 - Describes a situation where the ancestor's poverty was so severe that they resorted to begging, a highly stigmatized act in traditional Chinese society.
  • 此其子孙之所深讳 - Means 'this is something their descendants deeply conceal,' indicating a family's desire to hide a shameful past.
  • 暴之 - Means 'to expose' or 'to reveal,' suggesting that the speaker is considering making the family's past public.
  • 无乃不可乎 - A rhetorical question meaning 'wouldn't this be inappropriate?' or 'isn't this perhaps unacceptable?'
  • 愚山子曰 - A self-referential phrase meaning 'the Foolish Mountain Man says,' indicating the speaker's identity or persona.
  • 不然 - A negation meaning 'not so' or 'that is not the case.'
  • 行之可传而名 - Means 'actions that can be passed down and remembered,' emphasizing the importance of deeds over mere reputation.
  • 品之可尊而贵 - Means 'character that can be respected and honored,' highlighting the value of moral integrity.
  • 名与贵不关其所遭 - Means 'fame and honor are not determined by one's circumstances,' suggesting that these qualities are independent of external conditions.
  • 关其人之贤不肖也 - Means 'they depend on the person's virtue or lack thereof,' emphasizing the role of individual character.
  • 若翁之所行 - Means 'the actions of your ancestor,' referring to the ancestor's deeds.
  • 古之大贤 - Means 'a great sage of ancient times,' indicating a high level of moral and intellectual achievement.
  • 王氏子孙当世世师之 - Means 'the descendants of the Wang family should revere and follow him generation after generation,' suggesting a lasting legacy.
  • 乞宁足讳也 - Means 'begging is not something to be ashamed of,' challenging the notion that poverty and begging are inherently disgraceful.
  • 彼行之不道 - Means 'those who act unethically,' referring to people who engage in immoral behavior.
  • 操、惇、莽、卞、杞、桧之流 - Refers to specific historical figures known for their unethical or tyrannical actions, such as Cao Cao, Dong Zhuo, Wang Mang, Bian Liang, Qi Ji, and Qin Hui.
  • 真乞人之所不为 - Means 'truly, these are things that beggars would not do,' suggesting that even those in dire circumstances would not stoop to such low morals.
  • 而其子孙所羞以为祖父者 - Means 'and their descendants would be ashamed to have them as ancestors,' indicating the lasting shame associated with such actions.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 雷于粤为最远郡 - Geographical description meaning 'Lei is the most distant prefecture of Yue.' Requires contextual understanding of the region and administrative divisions during the Ming Dynasty.

  • 金陵人某以部曹出守 - Describes a Jinling person (from Nanjing) appointed as a governor, where '部曹' refers to a low-ranking official in the imperial bureaucracy and '出守' means to be appointed as a governor.
  • 舟入江遇盗 - Describes the situation where the official's boat was attacked by bandits on the river.
  • 知其守也，杀之，并歼其从者 - The bandits recognized the official's status and killed him along with his attendants.
  • 独留其妻女 - The bandits left the official's wife and daughter alive.
  • 以众中一最黠者为伪守 - They chose the most cunning person among them to impersonate the official.
  • 持牒往 - Means to carry a document or warrant, indicating the impersonator's attempt to maintain authority.
  • 抵郡逾月，甚廉干，有治状 - The impersonator governed the prefecture for over a month, appearing very diligent and efficient.
  • 雷人相庆得贤太守 - The people of Lei celebrated getting a good governor.
  • 其寮属暨监司使，咸诵重之 - The subordinates and the provincial inspector praised him highly.
  • 未几，太守出示禁游客 - Shortly after, the governor issued an order prohibiting visitors from entering the prefecture.
  • 所隶毋得纳金陵人只履 - His subordinates were forbidden from accepting any item from Jinling (Nanjing).
  • 否者虽至戚必坐 - Anyone who violated this order, even if they were close relatives, would be punished.
  • 诘朝守出，子道视，非父也 - The next day, when the governor went out, his son saw that he was not his father.
  • 讯其籍里名姓，则皆父 - When questioned, the governor provided the same information as his father.
  • 然不敢暴语，密以白监司使 - However, he did not openly speak about it and secretly informed the provincial inspector.
  • 于是戒吏，以卒环太守舍，而伏甲酒所 - He then ordered the officials to surround the governor's residence with soldiers and hid armed men in the place where the governor would be dining.
  • 旦日，太守入谒，监司饮之酒，出其子质，不辨也 - The next day, when the governor came to pay respects, the provincial inspector served him wine and brought out the son to confront him, but the governor could not distinguish him.
  • 守窘，拟起为变，而伏甲发，就坐捽之 - The governor became desperate and tried to cause trouble, but the hidden armed men attacked and seized him.
  • 其卒之环守者，亦破署入 - The soldiers surrounding the governor's residence also broke in.
  • 贼数十人，卒起格斗，胥逸去，仅获其七 - The bandits fought back, but most escaped, and only seven were captured.
  • 狱具如律，械送金陵杀之 - After the trial, the bandits were sentenced according to law, shackled, and sent to Jinling to be executed.
  • 于是雷之人乃知向之守，非守也，盗云 - Then the people of Lei realized that the previous governor was not a real governor but a bandit.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 杜先生岑尝言 - Mr. Du, a student of Mr. Cen, once said.
  • 归安茅止生习于高阳孙少师道公 - Mr. Mao Zhi from Gu'an learned from Mr. Sun Shao, the Master of the Way from Gaoyang.
  • 天启二年 - In the second year of Tianqi (1622).
  • 以大学士经略蓟辽 - As a Grand Councillor, he was sent to oversee Jizhou and Liaodong.
  • 置酒别亲宾 - He held a banquet to bid farewell to his relatives and guests.
  • 会者百人 - A hundred people attended.
  • 有客中坐，前席而言曰 - One of the guests sat forward and said.
  • 公之出，始吾为国庆，而今重有忧 - When you first left, I rejoiced for the nation, but now I am deeply concerned.
  • 封疆社稷，寄公一身 - The defense of the borders and the nation's safety are entrusted to you alone.
  • 公能堪，备物自奉，人莫之非 - If you can bear the burden, if you are well-provided for, no one will criticize you.
  • 如不能，虽毁身家，责难逭 - If you cannot, even if it means destroying your own family, the blame cannot be avoided.
  • 况俭觳乎 - How much more so if you are frugal and humble?
  • 吾见客食皆凿，而公独饭粗 - I see that all the guests are eating delicacies, while you alone eat coarse food.
  • 饰小名以镇物，非所以负天下之重也 - You are pretending to be modest to impress others, but this is not how to bear the weight of the nation.
  • 公揖而谢曰 - The official bowed and replied.
  • 先生诲我甚当，然非敢以为名也 - Your advice is very appropriate, but I do not do it for the sake of reputation.
  • 好衣甘食，吾为秀才时固不厌 - When I was a scholar, I enjoyed fine clothes and good food.
  • 自成进士，释褐而归，念此身已不为己有 - After becoming a Jinshi and returning home, I realized that my body no longer belongs to me.
  • 而朝廷多故，边关日骇 - The court is in turmoil, and the borders are in constant danger.
  • 恐一旦肩事任，非忍饥劳，不能以身率众 - I fear that if I take on responsibilities, I must endure hunger and labor to lead by example.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 天启二年 - The second year of the Tianqi era (1622) of the Ming Dynasty.
  • 大学士 - A high-ranking official in the Ming Dynasty, equivalent to a Grand Councillor.
  • 经略蓟辽 - To oversee military and administrative affairs in Jizhou and Liaodong, two important regions in northern China.
  • 置酒别亲宾 - A traditional Chinese practice of holding a farewell banquet for relatives and guests before a long journey or important mission.
  • 封疆社稷 - Refers to the defense of the borders and the nation's safety, emphasizing the official's responsibility.
  • 秀才 - A scholar who has passed the first level of the imperial examinations.
  • 释褐 - To leave official robes, meaning to retire from government service.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence contrasts the initial joy with the current concern, emphasizing the change in sentiment.
  • This phrase emphasizes the official's sole responsibility for the nation's defense.
  • This conditional clause presents a positive outcome if the official can bear the burden.
  • This negative conditional clause presents a severe consequence if the official cannot bear the burden.
  • This rhetorical question adds emphasis to the previous statement.
  • This sentence describes a contrast between the guests and the official, highlighting the official's frugality.
  • This sentence criticizes the official's behavior, suggesting that it is not appropriate for someone bearing the weight of the nation.
  • This phrase describes the official's polite response to the guest's criticism.
  • This sentence acknowledges the criticism while explaining the official's true motivation.
  • This sentence reflects on the official's past enjoyment of fine clothes and good food.
  • This sentence marks a turning point in the official's life, where he realizes that his body no longer belongs to him.
  • This sentence describes the current turbulent situation in the court and the constant danger at the borders.
  • This sentence explains the official's motivation for enduring hunger and labor to lead by example.
  • This sentence summarizes the official's self-discipline over the past nineteen years.

Proper Nouns

  • 杜先生岑 - Mr. Du, a student of Mr. Cen.
  • 归安茅止生 - Mr. Mao Zhi from Gu'an.
  • 高阳孙少师道公 - Mr. Sun Shao, the Master of the Way from Gaoyang.
  • 天启二年 - The second year of the Tianqi era (1622).
  • 大学士 - A high-ranking official in the Ming Dynasty.
  • 经略蓟辽 - To oversee military and administrative affairs in Jizhou and Liaodong.
  • 秀才 - A scholar who has passed the first level of the imperial examinations.
  • 释褐 - To leave official robes, meaning to retire from government service.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 呜呼 - Interjection expressing sorrow, lamentation, or deep emotion.
  • 公之气折逆奄 - "折" means to break or overcome; "逆奄" refers to a powerful and oppressive figure. This phrase suggests the subject's ability to overcome a tyrant or oppressive force.
  • 明周万事 - "明周" means to be clear and thorough; "万事" means all matters. Together, it implies being clear and thorough in all matters.
  • 合智谋忠勇之士以尽其材 - "合" means to gather; "智谋忠勇之士" refers to wise, strategic, loyal, and brave individuals; "尽其材" means to fully utilize their talents. This phrase suggests gathering such individuals to fully utilize their talents.
  • 用危困疮痍之卒以致其武 - "危困疮痍之卒" refers to soldiers in dire straits or suffering from wounds; "致其武" means to achieve their martial prowess. This phrase suggests using such soldiers to achieve their martial prowess.
  • 犹有伦比 - "伦比" means to be comparable. This phrase suggests that even among notable figures of the Tang and Song dynasties, there are few who can be compared.
  • 诚能动物 - "诚" means sincerity; "能动物" means to move or influence others. This phrase suggests that sincerity can move or influence others.
  • 所纠所斥 - "纠" means to correct; "斥" means to reprimand. Together, they refer to correction and reprimand.
  • 退无怨言 - "退" means to retreat or withdraw; "无怨言" means without complaint. This phrase suggests that those who are corrected or reprimanded do not complain.
  • 叛将远人 - "叛将" refers to rebel generals; "远人" refers to distant or foreign people. This phrase suggests that even rebel generals and distant people understand the subject's intentions.
  • 咸喻其志 - "咸" means all; "喻其志" means to understand their intentions. This phrase suggests that all understand the subject's intentions.
  • 革心无贰 - "革心" means to change one's heart or intentions; "无贰" means without hesitation. This phrase suggests that they change their hearts without hesitation.
  • 克己省身 - "克己" means to discipline oneself; "省身" means to reflect on oneself. Together, they refer to self-discipline and self-reflection.
  • 忧民体国 - "忧民" means to be concerned about the people; "体国" means to care for the state. Together, they refer to being concerned about the people and caring for the state.
  • 自然而忾乎天下 - "自然" means naturally; "忾乎天下" means to evoke a response throughout the world. This phrase suggests that such sincerity naturally evokes a response throughout the world.
  • 躬豪杰之才 - "躬" means to possess; "豪杰之才" means the talent of a hero or outstanding individual. This phrase suggests possessing the talent of a hero or outstanding individual.
  • 慨乎有闻于圣人之道 - "慨乎" means to be moved or inspired; "有闻于圣人之道" means to have heard of the way of the sage. This phrase suggests being moved or inspired by the way of the sage.
  • 唯公有焉 - "唯" means only; "公" refers to the subject; "有焉" means to possess it. This phrase suggests that only the subject possesses this quality.
  • 二三执政 - "二三" means a few; "执政" refers to those in power or high office. This phrase suggests a few high-ranking officials.
  • 与中枢边境 - "中枢" refers to the central government; "边境" refers to the border regions. This phrase suggests those in the central government and border regions.
  • 事同一体之人 - "事同一体" means to have the same purpose or interest; "之人" refers to those people. This phrase suggests those who share the same purpose or interest.
  • 实不能容 - "实" means truly; "不能容" means unable to tolerate. This phrase suggests that they truly cannot tolerate the subject.
  • 信及豚鱼 - "信" means trust or faith; "及" means to reach; "豚鱼" refers to pigs and fish, often used to represent the simplest of creatures. This phrase suggests that trust or faith can reach even the simplest of creatures.
  • 媢嫉之臣 - "媢嫉" means jealousy or envy; "之臣" refers to those officials. This phrase suggests jealous or envious officials.
  • 可不惧哉 - "可不惧" means how can one not fear; "哉" is an interjection. This phrase suggests that one should fear.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 初 - At first or initially, marking the beginning of a narrative.
  • 數嫁不終 - Repeatedly married without a lasting union, indicating a series of unsuccessful marriages.
  • 饒於財 - Abundant in wealth, describing her financial situation.
  • 我當嫁官人耳 - I will marry an official, expressing her aspiration.
  • 老仆乃伪为官人 - The old servant pretended to be an official, describing his deception.
  • 日取其财易酒食 - Daily, he took her wealth to exchange for wine and food, detailing his actions.
  • 交欢诸缇校者 - Socialized with the imperial guards, indicating his interactions with military personnel.
  • 故得始终不及于难 - Thus, he remained unscathed throughout the troubles, summarizing the outcome of his actions.
  • 后姚氏察知其伪 - Later, Yao discovered his deception, marking a turning point in the story.
  • 大哭，骂老仆，以手提其耳，啮其面 - She wept bitterly, scolded the old servant, pulled his ears with her hands, and bit his face, describing her reaction.
  • 面上痕常满 - His face was always scarred, indicating the lasting effect of her actions.
  • 及司徒公出视师 - When the Grand Tutor went out to inspect the army, marking a change in circumstances.
  • 乃以老仆为军官 - He was then appointed as a military officer, describing the new role.
  • 冠将军冠，服将军服 - He wore the general's hat and uniform, detailing his new appearance.
  • 以见姚氏，姚氏则大喜 - To see Yao, who was greatly pleased, describing her reaction to his new status.
  • 老仆入谢司徒公曰 - The old servant entered to thank the Grand Tutor, saying, introducing his speech.
  • 老仆嗜饮酒，今七年不饮酒 - The old servant loves drinking; now, for seven years, he has not drunk, explaining his self-imposed abstinence.
  • 此后愿日夜倍饮酒以偿之 - From now on, he wishes to drink double the amount day and night to make up for it, expressing his intention.
  • 饮酒积病，遂以死，年五十七 - Drinking accumulated illness, he eventually died at the age of fifty-seven, summarizing his fate.
  • 老仆有四子，其次尝犯军法当死 - The old servant had four sons; one of them once violated military law and should have been executed, introducing a new character and situation.
  • 诸大帅卜从善等，罗拜司徒公曰 - The various generals, including Bu Congshan, prostrated themselves before the Grand Tutor, saying, introducing their plea.
  • 非愿公绌法，乃军中欲请之以劝忠义也 - Not wishing to diminish the law, but the army desires to pardon him to encourage loyalty and righteousness, explaining their request.
  • 当是时，郭老仆之名播两河云 - At that time, the name of Old Gu spread throughout the two rivers, summarizing the impact of his story.
  • 铭曰 - The inscription says, introducing the concluding poem.
  • 汝士大夫之师，而乃居于奴 - You, a teacher of scholars and officials, yet you reside among the servants, expressing a moral judgment.
  • 奴乎奴乎，奴尚则有，士大夫卒无 - Servants, servants, servants still exist, but scholars and officials have finally disappeared, concluding with a lament.

Cultural and Historical Allusions
